title: Letters: When talking utilities, scrutinize executive salaries
text:
Nice article on the increasing cost of electricity, but something was missing that deeply needs to be in all articles such as this one ("Household electricity bills skyrocket").

Energy bill: A USA TODAY analysis found that electric bills are up $300 over five years.

Energy bill: A USA TODAY analysis found that electric bills are up $300 over five years.

There should have been a paragraph about executive compensation at these utilities, and the top tier of management. As an example, the average compensation of Anthony Alexander of First Energy in Ohio has been about $10 million the past three years.

These utilities cry poverty with every rate increase and have the ability to shut off customers' service. Let's paint the total picture in future reports. In these tough economic times, everything deserves added scrutiny, especially public money.

The article on soaring electric bills ignored the cost reduction in natural gas. Natural gas today is two-thirds the price of earlier years.

USA TODAY receives about 300 letters each day. Most arrive via e-mail, but we also receive submissions by postal mail and fax. We publish about 35 letters each week.

We often select comments that respond directly to USA TODAY articles or opinion pieces. Letters that are concise and make one or two good points have the best chance of being selected, as do letters that reflect the vibrant debate around the nation on a particular subject.

We aim to make the letters platform a place where readers, not just writers representing institutions or interest groups, have their say.

The technological breakthroughs that have provided the United States with a potential 80-year supply of low-cost natural gas is already saving consumers billions of dollars while reducing U.S. energy imports. If utilities could be assured that this supply will not be blocked by special interest groups, they could build large numbers of gas-fired electric plants. These plants are much less expensive to build, more reliable and dramatically lower on environmental impact than coal-fired plants. The cost of electricity generated by wind power and solar energy is one-and-a-half to four times more expensive.

Having spent 40 years working in businesses where energy costs were critical to the United States generating its own manufacturing products and providing high-paying, long-term jobs for our citizens, I urge all consumers to tell the president and Congress to refuse to be coerced by special interest groups that are more interested in their personal agendas than the long-term future of the economy and environment.

Robert W. Gallant; Midland, Mich.

A study on energy efficiency

USA TODAY reported that household electricity bills skyrocketed, citing a Bureau of Economic Analysis study indicating that the 2010 rate increases were the largest sustained increase since a run-up in electricity prices during the 1970s.

Analysis Group Inc. has completed a study that puts these numbers into perspective. Our study focused on the economic impacts of the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative during its first three years of implementation, and we found significant economic benefits, in large part as a result of the fact that the 10 states that participate used a significant portion of their funds to pay for energy efficiency measures to help lower consumers' electricity and other energy bills. RGGI has produced benefits totaling $1.6 billion in economic value for the 10 states, which are Maryland, New Jersey, Delaware, New York and the six New England states.

Electricity prices in these 10 states declined by 4% from 2008 to August 2011, compared with the average U.S. electricity prices, which increased over 3% during the same period. These differences reflect the impact of significant investments in energy efficiency as well as the impact of lower natural gas prices, which shows up in these 10 states more strongly than in other states. The energy efficiency investments over the past three years help reduce consumer energy bills by $1.3 billion.

Paul Hibbard and Susan Tierney; Analysis Group Inc.; Boston

title: Corrections and clarifications - USATODAY.com
text:
The following corrections and clarifications have been published by USA TODAY.

Money: A cover story Oct. 23 about DreamWorks Animation should have made it clear that Shrek 2 is the highest-grossing animated film of all time domestically, while Toy Story 3 is the highest-grossing animated film worldwide.

News: A story Oct. 26 about President Obama's interview with Rolling Stone misstated historian Douglas Brinkley's first name.

News: An item in the Oct. 22 world briefs about a car bomb explosion in Syria misstated that country's capital. It is Damascus.

Life: An Oct. 15 cover story on Jason Aldean incorrectly identified country singer Eric Church's home state. Church is from North Carolina.

News: A story Oct. 15 on compounding pharmacies misstated where William Blau of the University of North Carolina-Chapel Hill orders steroid pain injections. He does not use compounding pharmacies.

Sports: Oct. 15 editions omitted the winner of the Greater Hickory Classic in the golf results. Fred Funk won the Champions Tour tournament Oct. 14. The correct results can be found on Page 9C of the Oct. 16 newspaper.

News: A story Oct. 15 about teaching computer science to girls gave the incorrect year for the founding of the National Center for Women & IT. The correct year is 2004.

Life: Coverage Oct. 12 of Barbra Streisand's homecoming concert misidentified a Brooklyn street referenced by the singer. "The last time I sang solo in Brooklyn was on someone's stoop on Pulaski Street," she told the crowd at Barclays Center.

News: A story Oct. 11 about Vice President Biden's debate preparations misidentified Ron Klain. He is Biden's former chief of staff.

Money: An Oct. 9 story on France's tax policy failed to make clear Bernard Arnault's tax plans. He said he would remain a resident of France for tax purposes despite his request for Belgian nationality.

News: In an Oct. 9 1A story on changes in religion, the percentage of U.S. adults with no religion was incorrect. It is 19.6%.

News: The location of the American Heritage Center at the University of Wyoming was incorrect in a State-by-State item on Oct. 5. It is in Laramie.

News: AARP Foundation President Jo Ann Jenkins provided information on the organization's "Drive to End Hunger" in a story in the Oct. 2 Hunger in the USA section. Her first name was missing and her gender was incorrect.

News: An Oct. 2 cover story about an intentional plane crash inaccurately described the location of two engines on a Boeing 727, which are on the sides of the fuselage.

Life: The Nielsens cable top 10 chart Oct. 3 listed incorrect teams in several football games: No. 1 was Seahawks/Packers (ESPN); No. 2, Browns/Ravens (NFLN); and No. 9, Mississippi/Alabama (ESPN).

Sports: A listing in the college football coaches poll on Page 8C of Oct. 1 editions misstated the start time for No. 18 Oregon State's game Sept. 29 against Washington State. It begings at 6 p.m. ET.

Sports: A story in Oct. 1 editions incorrectly reported that the Houston Texans were trailing at halftime of their NFL game Sept. 30 with the Tennessee Titans. The Texans were leading 14-7.

News: An Oct.1 front page story on advertising in Senate races incorrectly described the U.S. Chamber of Commerce's spending in federal elections. It is advertising in congressional races only.

News: A chart accompanying an Oct. 1 cover story about Ross Perot misstated the size of the federal budget deficit as a percentage of the U.S. economy. It was 4.7% in 1992 and 8.5% in 2012.

News: A chart in the Sept. 28 cover story on increased water costs around the nation over the last decade listed an incorrect rise for Memphis. It is 52.7%

Money: A story Sept. 26 about new Nook HD tablets from Barnes & Noble should have said prices for the 8.9-inch Kindle Fire HD start at $299.

Sports: A story in Sept. 24's editions on baseball?s pennant races misidentified the 1951 team that overcame at least a six-game deficit with 14 games remaining. It was the New York Giants.

Sports: A story Sept. 24 about the USA TODAY Sports college football coaches poll incorrectly reported the highest-ranked Big 12 team and the number of Big 12 teams in the rankings. West Virginia is No. 7 and is the seventh team from the conference in the poll.

News: A photo caption in the 1A cover story Sept. 21 about the Midwest drought misstated the amount of corn on Dennis Green's farm in Lawrenceville, Ill., that was declared a total loss by insurance adjusters. It is 60 acres.

News: The location of Laramie Regional Airport was incorrect in the state-by-state report on Sept. 19. It is in Wyoming.

News: A story Sept. 20 on the possible discovery of the remains of England's King Richard III omitted the first name of British Member of Parliament Chris Skidmore.

Money: A chart on page 2B accompanying a cover story Sept. 17 about the 50th anniversaries of Walmart, Kmart and Target misstated each company's most recent annual sales. They are $15.3 billion for Kmart (2011), $68.5 billion for Target (2011), $443.9 billion for Walmart (2012).

Money: Friday's Test Drive should have said that starting prices for the 2013 Honda Accord coupe are $24,140 for the four-cylinder model, $31,140 for the V-6 model.

Life: A story Sept. 17 on bone marrow transplants incorrectly named Sally-Ann Roberts, a marrow donor for her sister, Good Morning America anchor Robin Roberts.

News: A story Sept. 14 on the service for former astronaut Neil Armstrong misstated John Glenn's achievement. Glenn was the third person, and first American, to orbit Earth.

Sports: A story in Sept.11 editions about the 2011 handshake involving NFL coaches Jim Schwartz and Jim Harbaugh had an incorrect given name for a former Israeli prime minister. He was Yitzhak Rabin.

News: In a Democratic convention story Sept. 5 on the Obama campaign's efforts to reach out to military veterans, the statuses of Mike Breen and Tyson Belanger were misstated. They are former military officers.

News: In some editions Sept. 4, a story on union activity over Labor Day weekend included information on a rally in Philadelphia that occurred in August.

News: A caption accompanying a photo from the Republican National Convention on 5A Aug. 29 misidentified Utah's governor. He is Gary Herbert.

News: The Republican National Convention was held in St. Paul in 2008. A story in the Aug. 24 paper incorrectly said it was held in Minneapolis.

Money: A chart Aug. 23 of historical bull markets for the Standard & Poors 500 index had incorrect beginning and end dates.The correct dates were published in the Aug. 24 print edition.

News: A North Carolina brief in an Aug. 20 Across the USA omitted which customer will see a reduction in their monthly bill because of a rate adjustment approved by the North Carolina Utilities Commission. They are Duke Energy customers.

News: The Colorado movie theater where James Holmes is accused of fatally shooting 12 people and injuring 57 is called Century 16. The theater was incorrectly identified in the Aug. 20 1A cover story.

News: A story Aug. 20 about drought conditions in the Farm Belt misstated the results of DuPont Pioneer's tests on its drought-resistant corn. Tests showed its yields were 7.1% higher in drought conditions and 3.4% higher in more favorable weather compared with other corn seeds.

Money: An Aug. 17 story on Jaguar's plans for 2013 failed to note that it once had an all-wheel-drive system, on X-type compact sedans and wagons.

Money: An Aug. 14 story on Google's purchase of Fodor's misnamed a feature in Apple's upcoming mobile operating system. It is called Passbook.

Sports: A story Aug. 15 about the Arkansas State football team incorrectly referenced a tweet about running back Michael Dyer's status. In a later tweet on the same account, Dyer said his page had been hacked and he was not transferring to Prairie View

Life: Author Vaddey Ratner attended Carleton College in Northfiled, Minn. The college was misspelled in a story August 9.

Life: Cirque du Soleil: World's Away is the first 3-D film in the company's 28-year history. A story August 9 was incorrect.

Sports: A story in August 2's editions on athletes using marijuana erroneously reported testing for pot was taken out of the NBA's collective bargaining agreement. In fact, the league tests only for performace enhancing substances during the offseason.

News: A story August 7 about increased attendance at gun instruction classes incorrectly identified a retired professor at Saint Louis University School of Law who has studied the issue of gun control. He is Don Kates.

News: The August 2 cover story about a movement to measure "gross national happiness" did not make clear how Bhutan limits its tourism. The country does not directly restrict the number of visitors, but it imposes other restrictions, such as issuing visas only to tourists booked with a local licensed tour operator.

Sports: In the high school football Super 25 rankings in August 2 editions, Jordan Smallwood's school's was incorrectly identified. He plays for Jenks (Okla.) High.

Life: Myra Breckinridge by Gore Vidal was published in 1968. The date was incorrect in an appreciation August 3.

Sports: In the preseason Super 25 prep football rankings in August 3 edition, Union (Tulsa) High's 2011 record was incorrect. Union finished 13-1.

Money: A story July 31 about shopping in London during the Olympics misstated the rate of value-added tax. It is 20% on most items.

Sports: In a column August 1 about Michael Phelps, the number of Summer Olympic gold medals won by Brazil was incorrect. Brazil has won 21.

News: A story August 1 about the Texas Senate runoff election misspelled the last name of the Democratic nominee in some editions. His name is Paul Sadler.

News: An item on the front page August 1 about the movie Total Recall misspelled the last name of actress Jessica Biel.

Money: In a story July 30 about the Five Guys hamburger chain, CEO Jerry Murrell's name was misspelled in one instance.

Money: A cover story about insurance advertising misidentified one company executive in some editions July 26. He is Chris Owen, vice president for marketing at USAA.

Life: A BBC America general manager was misidentified in a story July 25 about up-and-coming Brits in entertainment, the arts and pop culture. His name is Perry Simon.

News: A story July 25 about Colorado shooting suspect James Holmes quoted neuroscientist David Eagleman. He says he did not mean to suggest that all graduate students win research grants.

News: A story July 23 about the victims of the Aurora shooting misspelled the name of Gordon Cowden.

News: A story July 24 about insects breeding more during the summer heat should have said Missy Henrikson is with the National Pest Management Association.

News: A list accompanying a July 10 story on personal watercraft safety gave the incorrect minimum age required to ride one in Michigan. State law requires riders to be age 16 with a safety certificate, although there are exceptions for those who are 14 or 15, have a safety certificate and are supervised.

Life: Study findings were transposed in a story July 16 about mild cognitive impairment. The risk of death among people with the condition is double that of cognitively normal people; for people with Alzheimer's, it's three times higher than normal, according to a study by the Albert Einstein College of Medicine in New York.

News: A South Dakota item in Across the USA on July 12 misstated the weight of the bronze statue of Methuselah the Galapagos tortoise at Reptile Gardens in Rapid City. It weighs 360 pounds.

News: A 3A photo caption with Nationline on July 13 misidentified the location of a dog park. It is in Alabaster, Ala.

News: The federal share of cash welfare benefits was $5.3 billion in fiscal year 2011. A 1A story July 9 about states limiting how recipients may spend the money misstated that amount.

News: A July 6 Across the USA item misstated the location of the New Mexico State Fair. It is in Albuquerque.

News: A photo caption with July 11 1A cover story on Opana misstated the date of an attempted pharmacy robbery in Fort Wayne, Ind. It was Feb. 27.

News: July 3 1A cover story on those who die late in wars misstated the academic affiliation of retired Army brigadier general Casey Brower. He teaches at the Virginia Military Institute

News: A June 27 cover story on fracking misstated the funding source for a University of Texas environmental study by the Energy Institute. It was paid for with university funds.

News: The High Park wildfire in Colorado has burned more than 136 square miles. A story June 29 misstated its size.

News: A story June 28 on new Egyptian President Mohammed Morsi misidentified where he taught college. It was California State University-Northridge. The wrong information was provided by the Associated Press.

Life: In a story June 27 about metal-on-metal hip devices, a Food and Drug Administration official said the agency might consider more rigorous pre-market evaluation in the future. The type of regulation stated was incorrect.

Life: The date of June 27's cover story photo of Nora Ephron was incorrect. The photo was taken in 2006.

Life: A photo of Rod Stewart in the Lifeline column June 27 was incorrectly credited. The photographer is Penny Lancaster Stewart.

Sports: A story June 25 about the women's 100 meters incorrectly stated that no dead heat had occured before this year for an Olympic berth in the U.S. trials. There were two instances of dead heats, one in 1952 in the men's 100 and one in 1980 in the men's 400 hurdles.

Money: A headline June 22 on a cover story about Buick mischaracterized the image issue Buick is trying to address. It is updating its lineup to appeal to younger, import-oriented buyers.

Money: A story on Wi-Fi in hotels June 21 misstated the time period that Internet provider iBahn saw the amount of data hotel guests downloaded per session jump 50%. It was between March and December 2011.

News: A news brief in June 22 Nationline on turmoil at the University of Virginia incorrectly identified the school's location. It is in Charlottesville.

News: A Newsline photo on 1A June 21 misidentified former U.S. senator Birch Bayh, D-Ind.

News: A story June 20 about wildfires in the West gave the wrong location for the Little Bear Fire. It's near Ruidoso, N.M.

News: A cover story June 13 on school bullying misstated the role of Rosalind Wiseman in a 2010 Massachusetts case. Wiseman worked with prosecutors before and after the case involving Phoebe Prine, but she did not consult with them while the case was active.

News: A story June 13 about harassment of women in Egypt and the Middle East misidentified a U.S. group founded by Holly Kearl to help fight the problem. The group is Stop Street Harassment.

Money: TriWest Healthcare Alliance submitted a lower bid for the military's Tricare contract than its main competitor. A story June 8 indicated otherwise.

Sports: A story in June 8 editions about Seattle's reaction to the city's former team, the Oklahoma City Thunder, reaching the NBA Finals incorrectly stated which municipalities are trying to get a bond passed to build a new arena and the amount of money needed. The city of Seattle and King County are seeking $120 million if an NBA team is placed there and an additional $80 million for an NHL team.

News: A story June 8 about New York City's "Stop and Frisk" policy incorrectly stated that Rep. Yvette Clarke, D-N.Y., met with the Justice Department on June 7 about that issue.

Life: Sona Mehring, founder and CEO of the CaringBridge health website, was incorrectly identified in a photo caption June 6.

Life: Justin Cronin revisits the post-apocalyptic world of vampires in The Twelve, to be published in October. The novel's subject matter was incorrect in a story June 7.

Money: A story June 7 should have said that auto-industry forecaster LMC Automotive believes that pure electric vehicles, excluding plug-in hybrids, will account for 1% of new vehicle sales in 2020.

Money: Research firm IDC forecasts that as many as 180 million people in the U.S. will play online games by the end of 2015. A story June 1 about changes in the video game industry didn't make it clear that the forecast pertained only to online games.

Money: Subway supplied incorrect information on the nutritional criteria for its American Heart Association Heart-Check seal, the subject of a story June 1. The chart with the story should have said the criteria for saturated fat, cholesterol and sodium were per 100 grams, not per meal. To meet the criteria, the total fat should be less than 3 grams per 100 grams of the meal.

News: An Across the USA item June 1 about the I-35W bridge collapse in Minneapolis in 2007 incorrectly ran under a Wisconsin heading.

Money: Due to a production error, a markets story with outdated economic data appeared on 1B in some editions of the June 1 Money section.

Sports: A story in some editions May 31 misidentified the team receiving the Brooklyn Nets' first-round pick in this year's NBA draft. It was the Portland Trail Blazers.

Sports: A news brief in some editions May 31 about Brazil defeating the USA in a men's exhibition soccer game had an incorrect attendance figure. It should have been 67,619.

Sports: The National League roundup misidentified the team playing the San Francisco Giants on May 30 in some editions May 31. The Arizona Diamondbacks were the opponent and won 4-1.

Life: Walter Cronkite went to work for CBS in 1950. The year was incorrect in a story May 24.

Life: A story May 24 gave the incorrect year for Jaco Pastorius' death. The jazz virtuoso died in 1987.

Life: A photo caption May 24 gave the wrong date for the assassination of John F. Kennedy. It was No. 22, 1963.

News: In a story May 24 on agreements to limit negative campaign ads, Rep. Denny Rehberg's first name was misspelled.

Sports: The TV Sports column May 18, in noting MLB-owned MLB Networ will this year air MLB playoff coverage, incorrectly overlooked that two league-owned channels — NBA TV and NHL Network— carry postseason games.

Life: Bruce Willis' character was incorrectly identified May 15 in a photo caption about the Cannes Film Festival. Willis plays Captain Sharp in Moonrise Kingdom.

Money: A photo caption May 15 on a cover story about CEOs under fire misidentified the company from which Scott Thompson has stepped down as CEO. It was Yahoo.

News: A 1A story May 14 about consumer debt and savings misattributed a quote. Gerri Detweiler, director of consumer education for Credit.com, said, "They still have debt to deal with before they can take care of things like saving more money or paying for their kids' college education."

Money: A May 7 story, "Insurers Embrace 'Virtual' Doctor Visits," described how a 2010 ruling by the Texas Medical Board effectively blocks a physician from treating new patients via telemedicine. The rule restricts physicians from treating new patients from the patients' homes. It does provide for a virtual consultation, however, if that first-time patient goes to "an established medical setting," such as a clinic, EMS station or pharmacy equipped with diagnostic tools and which is staffed by a nurse or pharmacist who could help the physician make a physical evaluation of the patient.

News: A story May 8 about voter views on climate change failed to clarify that Sen. John McCain, R-Ariz., opposed climate change-related legislation in 2009 but is open to other proposals to address global warming.

News: A quote in a 1A story May 9 about the foiled al-Qaeda plot to blow up a U.S. airliner was misattributed. Former Homeland Security secretary Michael Chertoff said, "You have to look at security as a series of layers. No one layer is flawless or foolproof."

News: A story May 9 about the defeat of Sen. Richard Lugar gave the incorrect party affiliation for Sen. Lisa Murkowski of Alaska. She won re-election as a write-in Republican.

Money: A photo caption May 8 accompanying the cover story on European elections and the economy mistranslated a sign carried by a demonstrator in France. The sign read "Don't Disappoint Us."

Sports: An item in the Major League Baseball power rankings published May 8 incorrectly listed the maximum amount of runs the Philadelphia Phillies scored in their 11 most recent defeats. They had scored four runs or fewer in nine of their 11 most recent defeats.

Life: A story May 4 about viewing the May 20 solar eclipse from national parks referred incorrectly to the location of Canyon de Chelly National Monument. It is in northeast Arizona.

News: A May 4 cover story on colorful town names misspelled the name of the English poet who wrote The Faerie Queene. He was Edmund Spenser.

Sports: A headline in some editions May 2 misidentified the team the Chicago Bulls lost to on May 1. It was the Philadelphia 76ers.

News: A story April 30 on common education standards incorrectly listed Arkansas instead of Alaska as one of the four states that has not adopted Common Core standards.

News: A story April 30 on the death of Osama bin Laden misstated the destination of the flight that the "underwear bomber" was on. It was Detroit.

Sports: A list of winners from the U.S. Olympic wrestling trials in the April 24 edition incorrectly identified the freestyle champion at 121 pounds. He is Sam Hazewinkel of Norman, Okla.

News: A photo on page 4A April 24 of William Vinsko was incorrectly identified as Rep. Lou Barletta.

Sports: An item in April 20 editions listing five memorable moments at Fenway Park misstated the historical context of Game 6 of the 1975 World Series. It was the latest-ending Series night game to that point, finishing at 12:34 a.m.

News: A Miltary Times story April 18 on back federal taxes owed by U.S. troops misstated the amount owed. The total is $340 million.

Opinion: An editorial April 18 on the Secret Service scandal misstated the capital of Colombia. It is Bogota, not Cartagena.

Opinion: An editorial April 16 on housing incorrectly said that Edward DeMarco, acting director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency, had been appointed by President George W. Bush. Although DeMarco held senior positions in housing finance during the Bush administration, it was as a career civil servant.

Money: A story April 16 on Boeing's new version of its 737 passenger jet should have said that Southwest Airlines has bought 737s exclusively for its fleet, although it has operated a few Boeing 727s in the past.

News: In some editions April 18, a story on churches and technology gave the wrong name for an application for confession. The correct name is Confession: A Roman Catholic App.

Sports: The given name for Bubba Watson's mom was misspelled in April 9 editions. Her name is Molly.

Sports: The Seattle Mariners shortstop who didn't play April 8 because of a stiff neck was incorrectly identified in April 9's team note. The shortstop was Brendan Ryan.

Sports: In the April 6 edition, the name of the Cincinnati Reds' affiliate in the Southern League was incorrect. The team is the Pensacola (Fla.) Blue Wahoos.

Sports: A March 14 story about uniform and sneaker suppliers to the 68 teams in the NCAA men's basketball tournament referred incompletely to the various jersey/shoe combinations. Five teams are outfitted by Russell Athletic and another by Crons, each of which wear Nike sneakers.

News: A story April 4 on the tornadoes near Dallas incorrectly attributed a statement that the storms surprised some residents. The source of the statement was Dallas County Judge Clay Jenkins.

Life: A story April 3 on The Shops at Target misstated the number of pieces in the collection. It contains nearly 400 pieces. The Webster portion of the collection contains 211 pieces.

Money: A story March 26 on a proposed London airport in the Thames River Estuary should have made it clear that Conservative British Prime Minister David Cameron heads a coalition government of Conservatives and Liberal Democrats.

Sports: A schedule March 27 for the NCAA Division I men?s ice hockey tournament referred incorrectly to the April 5 semifinal match-ups. Union faces Ferris State and Minnesota takes on Boston College.

Sports: A March 14 story about uniform and sneaker suppliers to the 68 teams in the NCAA men?s basketball tournament referred incompletely to the various jersey/shoe combinations. Five teams are outfitted by Russell Athletic and another by Crons, each of which wear Nike sneakers.

Sports: A cover story March 26 about Kansas reaching the NCAA men?s Final Four included an incorrect final score in some editions for the team?s earlier tournament victory against Purdue. The Jayhawks won 63-60. Also, the regional won by Ohio State was misidentified. It was the East.

Sports: A story March 26 about the Kentucky-Louisville men's basketball rivalry referred incorrectly in some editions to the 1959 NCAA regional semifinal won by Louisville. It was the Mideast.

Sports: A promotional key March 26 to an online story about the death of boxing writer Bert Sugar misstated his age in some editions. He was 75.

News: A 1A story in some editions March 23 incorrectly attributed a quote about Whitney Houston's toxicology report to NBA announcer Bill Walton's Twitter account. The tweet came from a fake Walton feed.

Sports: A listing March 19 showing this week?s NCAA Division II men?s basketball tournament games misidentified the team facing Montevallo on March 21. It is Metro State.

Sports: A news brief March 19 about results from Bellator 61 included an incorrect given name in some editions for a lightweight mixed martial artist. He is James Vick.

Life: Andrew Garfield was misidentified in a photo caption for The Amazing Spider-Man with a story March 15.

Sports: A team preview in March 16 editions incorrectly reported Detroit's most recent NCAA men's basketball tournament appearance. It lost in the second round to Ohio State in 1999.

Sports: ABC?s coverage of the March 15 NBA game between Boston and the L.A. Lakers was up 88% from comparable coverage of a Phoenix-Orlando game last year. Incorrect information was supplied to USA TODAY for the March 17 ratings chart.

Sports: A hockey story in the March 17 paper misidentified the player who eased up on a hit against Montreal's P.K. Subban. It was the Boston Bruins' Shawn Thornton.

News: A quote in story March 12 about increased ridership on mass transit was misattributed. Michael Melaniphy, president of the American Public Transportation Association, said, "It's not just an urban thing. When you look at small, rural parts of the country, cities under 100,000, the ridership increase was 5.4%, basically double the national average."

Life: A report showing that 61% of girls polled are ambivalent about leadership or success was conducted by the Girl Scout Research Institute. The institute was incorrectly identified in a story March 8.

Life: A story March 9 misidentified the site of Michel Richard's Las Vegas restaurant. It is at the Caesars Palace resort.

Money: A story March 7 about pet health care insurance misstated the ownership of Banfield Pet Hospital. It is a subsidiary of Mars, Inc. In the U.S. it operates mostly within PetSmart stores.

Money: Employers requesting credit histories of potential employees do not get the individual's credit score. A story March 6 about employers using credit reports when hiring was unclear on that point.

News: A story March 6 on iPad accessory makers misquoted TouchFire's Steve Isaac on how long it took his company to raise $200,000 using the website Kickstarter. It took seven weeks.

Sports: A cover story and accompanying chart March 6 about pro coaches' tenure included an incorrect term to describe some numbers. The median average was used.

Money: A story March 5 about online-storage company Dropbox misstated CEO Drew Houston's academic record. He is a graduate of Massachusetts Institute of Technology.

Sports: A cover story March 5 about the NFL?s probe into a bounty system that rewarded hits that resulted in injuries included an incorrect surname for a former New Orleans Saints defensive end. He is Bobby McCray.

Life: A story March 5 about Amnesty International's Secret Policeman's Ball incorrectly misidentified the North Korean leader whom Rex Lee portrayed in a sketch with Jon Stewart. It was Kim Jong Un. The story also misspelled British comedian David Walliams' last name.

Life: A story March 5 on new TV dramas being developed for fall, a potential CBS series based on 1960s Las Vegas sheriff Ralph Lamb should have described him as a former rodeo cowboy.

Life: A March 1 appreciation for Davy Jones incorrectly identified the broadcaster for the soap opera Coronation Street, where Jones got his start as an actor. It's aired by ITV Granada. Though members of The Monkees played a variety of instruments, the lineup most frequently featured Peter Tork on bass, Michael Nesmith on guitar, Micky Dolenz on drums and Jones on vocals and tambourine.

Sports: A listing Feb. 28 of mid-season NBA player statistics omitted Kevin Durant of the Oklahoma City Thunder. The full team?s numbers can be found on page 7C in the March 1 paper.

Sports: A story Feb. 29 about the drug-testing dispute involving Ryan Braun of the Milwaukee Brewers referred incorrectly in some editions to Boyd Johnson?s law firm. Johnson, who represents drug-test collector Dino Laurenzi Jr., is a partner with WilmerHale.

News: A story Feb 28 about airline pilot qualifications should have said the Federal Aviation Administration is proposing additional training, with 50 hours in the cockpit, to pilot planes with more than one engine. Co-pilots will need 1,500 hours of flying for an Airline Transport Pilot certificate rather than the current 250 hours for a Commercial Pilot certificate.

Sports: A news brief Feb. 28 about the death of high school football player Rico Webb referred incorrectly to the reported cause. He had a blood clot that reached his lungs.

Money: A story Feb. 27 about the Mobile World Congress in Barcelona erroneously described the AT&T version of the HTCOne X. It is a dual-core phone. Also, the story misstated the maximum projection image of the Samsung Galaxy Beam smartphone. It is 50 inches wide.

Money: A map accompanying a story Feb. 24 about a shale energy boom incorrectly named a state bordering West Virginia. It is Kentucky.

Opinion: A letter Feb. 22 should have stated that defense research funding represents 2% of the total federal budget, not the defense budget.

News: A headline in some editions Feb. 22 on a story about a cancer drug shortage misstated the drug to be imported from India under a Food and Drug Administration deal to address the shortage. It is Lipodox.

Sports: A news brief Feb. 20 about World Cup track cycling in London referred incorrectly to American Sarah Hammer's result. She won the women's omnium.

Sports: A list of scores Feb. 20 for the PGA Tour?s Northern Trust Open omitted the Feb. 19 final round numbers. (They can be found on page 11C in the Feb. 21 paper.)

News: A North Carolina brief in a Feb. 15 Across the USA misstated the location of Pigeon Forge. It is in Tennesee.

News: Investors in Home Instead Senior Care franchises can earn a yield ratio of 18.8, meaning an investment of $57,050 can bring $1,072,580 in annual revenue. A story about home health care workers Feb. 16 reported a different statistic.

Opinion: A Feb. 15 editorial on the school dropout age should have said the unemployment rate, not the employment rate, dropped to 8.3% in January.

Opinion: A Feb. 16 editorial on money market funds should have said that the government hasn't done enough, in our view, to prevent a repeat of the 2008 panic, not that the government hasn't acted.

Sports: A headline Feb. 16 with a story about the trade prospects of NHL forward Rick Nash misidentified his team. It is the Columbus Blue Jackets.

Money: Once the IRS has filed a substitute return on behalf of a taxpayer, it generally has 10 years to collect unpaid taxes. The Feb. 8 Your Money column contained incorrect information about the statute of limitations on collections when the IRS files a substitute return.

News: A story Feb. 14 about President Obama's budget should have said it failed to cut the $1.4 trillion 2009 deficit in half by 2013, as previously promised. The amount of his promised cut was omitted.

Life: Some editions Feb. 13 incorrectly identified Adele as the sixth artist to win six Grammy Awards in a night. She was the sixth to win the top three categories (album, song and record of the year) and the first to do so since the Dixie Chicks in 2007.

Sports: A story Feb. 9 about New York Knicks guard Jeremy Lin misstated the number of games he has started. It is two. Also, the story referred imprecisely to the postseason berth Lin led Harvard to in the 2009-10 season. It was the CollegeInsider.com tournament. In some editions, Lin also was mistakenly identified as the NBA's first Asian-American player. Wat Misaka, an American-born player of Japanese descent, was a guard for the Knicks in 1947-48, when the league was called the Basketball Association of America. Lin is the first American-born Chinese or Taiwanese player.

Sports: A story Feb. 9 about next week's U.S. Olympic women's boxing trials referred incorrectly in some editions to the dates of the world championships. They are May 9-20 in China.

Sports: A story Feb. 7 about Olympics held in the United States referred incorrectly to funds the U.S. Olympic Committee receives from the International Olympic Committee. The USOC receives 20% of global sponsor money and 12.75% of the U.S. Olympic broadcast rights fees.

Sports: A chart in some editions Feb. 6 misidentified the year in which New England Patriots quarterback Tom Brady was drafted in the sixth round. It was 2000.

Life: Former Van Halen bassist Michael Anthony was identified with the wrong first name in the Feb. 7 Listen Up review of the group's new album, A Different Kind of Truth.

News: King George VI died at Sandringham, northeast of London. A cover story Feb. 6 on Queen Elizabeth II misstated the location.

Opinion: A column by Aimee Phan on cutbacks and elimination of ethnic studies programs should not have included California State University, Los Angeles.

Money: A story Feb. 6 on Super Bowl ads should have said the advertising agency Doritos' ad contest winner will work with is Lonely Island.

Sports: A story Feb. 3 about NBA scoring referred incorrectly to the number of times a team had scored 100 points or more this season. It was 211, compared to 334 for the same period last season.

Sports: A list Feb. 3 of New England Patriots players who were on the team's roster for Super Bowl XLII in 2008 mistakenly included one player and omitted another in some editions. Logan Mankins was on the team; Deion Branch was not.

Sports: A chart Feb. 3 of NASCAR Sprint Cup crew chief changes for 2012 misstated in some editions Shane Wilson 's previous role. Wilson, now with driver Kevin Harvick, had been Clint Bowyer?s crew chief.

Sports: A list Feb. 3 of women's college basketball scores had an incorrect result in some editions for the Gonzaga-Portland game. Gonzaga won 77-56.

News: The Ohio item in the Feb. 1 Across the USA did not make clear how pets are buried with their owners at Standing Rock Cemetery in Kent. A separate section of the cemetery is set aside where cremated pet remains can be interred on top their owner's caskets.

News: A story and graphic Feb. 2 about super PACs listed an incorrect total for contributions received by the pro-Mitt Romney group Restore Our Future. It received $30,179,653 during 2011, which made it the No. 1 super PAC in contributions.

Life: A photo caption Feb. 2 for a story about Banana Republic's Mad Men collection gave the incorrect name for the show's costume designer. She is Janie Bryant.

Money: A story Jan. 31 on New York's congested airspace misstated the location of Stewart International Airport. It's in Orange County, N.Y.

News: A story Jan. 30 on deceased pet preservation misidentified the cable television show that features pets being preserved by freeze-drying. It is American Stuffers.

News: A story Jan. 30 about the previous week's solar storm gave an incorrect source for the accurate predicted time of the storm. It was NASA.

News: A story Jan. 27 about community colleges' plans to retrain older workers had an incorrect date for the scheduled release of the proposed 2013 federal budget. It will be released Feb. 13.

Money: A review of the Toyota Venza on Jan. 27 misstated where it is built, which is Georgetown, Ky.

Life: A Lifeline item Jan. 26 incorrectly listed Hugo as an animated film.

News: A world map Jan. 26 accompanying a story on kidnapped Americans was mislabeled. It identifies in red places where the State Department warns Americans of kidnapping danger.

Life: The Countess of Carnarvon is the author of Lady Almina and the Real Downton Abbey: The Lost Legacy of Highclere Castle. Her title was misstated in a review Jan. 26.

Life: Newbery Medal winner Jack Gantos is the author of a 2002 memoir called Hole in My Life. The title was incorrect in a story Jan. 24.

Sports: A story Jan. 26 on the 10 most hyped Super Bowls misidentified the coach of the Dallas Cowboys in Super Bowl XXVIII. It was Jimmy Johnson.

Sports: A story in Jan. 23 editions misidentified a new member of the Colorado Rockies. He is Ramon Hernandez.

News: Newbery Medal winner Jack Gantos is the author of a 2002 memoir called Hole in My Life. The title was incorrect in a story Jan. 24.

News: A story Jan. 24 about tornadoes in Alabama cited an incorrect date for an outbreak of twisters last year that killed 238 people. The tornadoes struck April 27.

News: A 1A story on CEO pay Jan. 24 did not make clear that $21.4 million of what Walt Disney CEO Robert Iger received in 2011 was based on stock options and restricted shares awarded in prior years. Iger also received $31.4 million in compensation for 2011.

Money: A story Jan. 20 about Eastman Kodak filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy described Ricoh incorrectly. The camera company is an independent corporation, not a division of IBM. In the same story, a photo caption should have stated that Onex purchased Kodak's health imaging business in 2007, creating a new company called Carestream Health.

Sports: The Deals column Jan. 18 referred incorrectly in some editions to Florida's National League team. It is the Miami Marlins.

Money: A story Jan. 17 should have said that even though this year's tax filing deadline is April 17, IRS offices will be open April 16.

Money: A story Jan. 17 about Zapos being hacked omitted a reference to the fact that stolen customer passwords were cryptographically scrambled.

Sports: A story Jan. 13 about boxer Robert Guerrero's desire to fight Floyd Mayweather Jr. included an incorrect knockout total for Mayweather in some editions. He is 42-0 with 26 KOs.

Life: Publisher Grand Central has moved the on-sale date of the novel Monday Mornings by Sanjay Gupta to March 13. The date was listed incorrectly Jan. 12.

Life: In the Jan. 12 print editions, "Pooches take the pulpit" was missing a byline. It was written by freelancer Sharon L. Peters.

Sports: A photo caption Jan. 12 accompanying a story about NHL fighting included a misspelled surname for a Phoenix Coyotes left wing in some editions. He is Paul Bissonnette.

Opinion: The opposing view in the Jan. 12 Super PAC debate incorrectly described a pro-Obama organization that ran ads against Mitt Romney. It is called Priorities USA Action, and it does disclose its donors.

Sports: A college football story Jan. 11 misidentified the hometown of former New Orleans Saints quarterback Bobby Hebert in some editions. He is a native of Cut Off., La.

Sports: A hockey item Jan. 9 misidentified the team that drafted goaltender Roberto Luongo. He was selected by the New York Islanders.

News: A photo caption accompanying a story Jan. 6 on Pentagon spending priorities misidentified Gen. James Amos, commandant of the U.S. Marine Corps, who was to the right of President Obama.

Sports: A hockey item Jan. 9 misidentified the team for which Matt Niskanen plays. He plays for the Pittsburgh Penguins.

Sports: A list Jan. 4 of men's college basketball scores included an incorrect result in some editions for the Missouri-Oklahoma game. Missouri won 87-49.

Sports: Two NBA news items Jan. 5 referred incorrectly to Golden State Warriors guard Stephen Curry's injury. He sprained his surgically repaired right ankle for the thirst assume this is third time this season.

Sports: A story Jan. 5 about West Virginia's Orange Bowl victory against Clemson misstated the number of times Clemson had appeared in that bowl. It was the school's fourth Orange Bowl and the first since its win against Nebraska in 1982.

News: A Jan. 4 1A cover story about military families gave the incorrect first name of Army Gen. Martin Dempsey, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff.

Sports: A promotional key Jan. 3 to a story looking at this season's 12 NFL playoff teams referred incorrectly in some editions to the Roman numeral label for next month's Super Bowl. It is XLVI.

Sports: A story Jan. 3 about NFL records set this season misstated in some editions the all-purpose yardage total for Darren Sproles of the New Orleans Saints. He had 2,696 yards.

Sports: The college bowls chart Jan. 3 included an incorrect score for the Music City Bowl in some editions. Mississippi State defeated Wake Forest 23-17.

Life: A story Dec. 30 about travel writers' favorite destinations misstated the number of institutions connected to the Chautauqua movement. There are 14 in North America.

News: A 1A cover story on Dec. 28 about former West Point cadets buried at the U.S. Military Academy cemetery misstated a distinction held by Lt. Emily Perez, who was killed in Iraq in 2006. When she was appointed Brigade Command sergeant major, she became the highest-ranked black and Hispanic female cadet in academy history.

Life: A Dec. 30 story about travel writers' favorite destinations misstated the number of institutions connected to the Chautauqua movement. There are 14 in North America.

Sports: A story Dec. 30 about the Philadelphia Flyers' 4-2 victory against the Pittsburgh Penguins misstated in some editions the number of Penguins who had more points than Flyers winger Jaromir Jagr. Evgeni Malkin and James Neal have 43 and 36 points, respectively, compared with the 31 of Jagr, himself a former Penguin.

Sports: A news brief Dec. 30 about the Michigan men's basketball victory against Penn State referred incorrectly to the Michigan nickname. It is the Wolverines.

Life: An item on Dec. 29 listing some of Andrew Harper's Hideaway Report 2012 Grand Award winners should have said that Australia's Southern Ocean Lodge was named Hideaway of the Year and that Manhattan's Ai Fiori is Gastronomic Restaurant of the Year.

Life: A bridal photograph with a story Dec. 27 about The Magic Room by Jeffrey Zaslow was credited incorrectly. It was taken by KellyLynne Photography.

News: A headline Dec. 29 on a story about the government health care program for the poor in Connecticut misstated its name. It is Medicaid.

News: A 1A story Dec. 29 on foreign investments in Iraq inaccurately described Hill International's contract with the Iraq government for construction of a sports arena. The contract to manage construction is valued at $3.3 million.

Sports: A TV column Dec. 28 misspelled the name of Southern California football coach Lane Kiffin in some editions.

Money: A story Dec. 28 should have said five lawsuits against a Denver pain management doctor were settled out of court. The cases involved two deaths and three patients who suffered brain injuries.

News: Dec. 28's 1A cover story about U.S. Military Academy graduates who died in the Iraq War misspelled the last name of a former cadet's fiancee, Marie Cicerelle.

News: A story Dec. 28 on bird behavior that inspired the Alfred Hitchcock movie The Birds gave an incorrect affiliation for plankton expert Raphael Kudela. He is professor at the University of California-Santa Cruz.

Sports: An item Dec. 27 about StubHub ticket prices for NBA games listed an incorrect matchup. Tickets were available at $5.50 for today's game between the Washington Wizards and Atlanta Hawks.

News: The New Mexico item Dec. 21 in Across the USA incorrectly listed a report from Flagstaff, Ariz.

Life: In some editions of the Dec. 21 Life section, the network that airs American Horror Story was misidentified. It is FX.

Sports: A snapshot Dec. 21 showing quarterbacks with the most consecutive games throwing a touchdown pass included an incorrect number for Johnny Unitas in some editions. He had 47.

Sports: A story Dec. 21 about Japanese pitcher Yu Darvish mistakenly included outfielder Hideki Matsui among players who had come to the major leagues through the posting system. Matsui was an unrestricted free agent when he was signed by the New York Yankees.

Sports: A column Dec. 22 about the Baseball Writers' Association of America's response to allegations of sexual abuse against columnist Bill Conlin referred imprecisely to a statement made in the Penn State abuse case. When then-Penn State president Graham Spanier used the term "groundless" to describe charges that had been made, he was referring to those made against university colleagues Tim Curley and Gary Schultz.

Sports: A story Dec. 21 about gymnast Nastia Liukin included an incorrect age. She is 22.

Sports: The West Region Super 25 high school football rankings Dec. 20 omitted No. 2 De La Salle of Concord, Calif., altering the remaining spots on the top 10 list.The corrected list was published Dec. 21 on page 9C.

Sports: The TV ratings column Dec. 20 misidentified the date of the Syracuse-North Carolina State men's basketball game, won by Syracuse. It was Dec. 17.

Sports: A news brief Dec. 19 about teams in the USA TODAY Super 25 that won state football championships misstated the number of consecutive Texas 4A titles won by Lake Travis (Austin). It is five.

Sports: The USA TODAY/ESPN men's college basketball coaches poll Dec. 13 included an incorrect score for the Syracuse-Marshall game. Syracuse won 62-56.

News: The North Dakota item in the Dec. 14 Across the USA inadvertently contained information from another state.

Sports: A timeline Dec. 9 of highlights in the Army-Navy rivalry misidentified the Army player known as the "lonesome end" in the 1958 game. He is Bill Carpenter.

Sports: The college football scores and schedules Dec. 6 included an incorrect result for Temple in its game against Kent State. Temple won 34-16.

Sports: A capsule Dec. 9 previewing the Chicago Bears-Denver Broncos match-up misstated the Broncos' record going into the game. It was 7-5, as was Chicago's.

Sports: A story Dec. 8 profiling accused child molester Jerry Sandusky misidentified a Washington, Pa., attorney who described his friendship with the former Penn State assistant football coach. He is Bill Mitchell.

Sports: A story Dec. 7 about the 2011-12 NBA schedule misstated in some editions the number of teams that would be playing three games in three days twice during the season. It is 11.

News: A Wyoming item in Across the USA on Dec. 5 misidentified a university. Landmark degrees were issued by University of Wyoming.

Sports: The Deals list Dec. 5 included conflicting information about NHL forward Chris Mueller. He was recalled by the Nashville Predators from the Milwaukee Admirals of the American Hockey League.

Sports: A story Dec. 5 about how coaches voted in the last USA TODAY college football poll of the regular season misidentified coach Nick Saban's team. It is Alabama.

Sports: A story Dec. 2 about notable postseason college football rematches referred incorrectly to the date of the 1959 LSU-Mississippi regular-season game. It was in October.

News: A story Dec. 2 about theaters providing seats for patrons to live-tweet about performances misstated the policy at Writers' Theatre in Glencoe, Ill. It does not allow tweeting.

News: A 1A story Nov. 30 on rising post-traumatic stress disorder cases among Iraq and Afghanistan veterans incorrectly stated a date for the data's release. The information is available upon request and will be posted on the Veterans Affairs' website within weeks.

News: Elissa Kim is the executive vice president of recruitment for Teach for America. Her affiliation was incorrect in an article on college volunteering in the Sharing in the USA section Nov. 29.

News: Clarke University was incorrectly spelled in the Iowa item in Nov. 25's Across the USA.

Sports: The golf results Nov. 28 omitted the winner of the Australian PGA Championship. Greg Chalmers defeated Robert Allenby and Marcus Fraser on the first hole of a playoff.

Sports: A story Nov. 28 about the free-agent prospects of outfielder Carlos Beltran referred incorrectly in some editions to one of the teams pursuing him. It is the Miami Marlins, who formally adopted a name change earlier this month.

Sports: A news brief Nov. 28 about the Baylor women's basketball victory against Tennessee omitted the given name of a Baylor player in some editions. She is Jordan Madden.

Sports: The listing Nov. 28 of NFL results included an incorrect score for the New York Jets-Buffalo Bills game in some editions. The Jets won 28-24.

Sports: A story Nov. 28 about the Atlanta Falcons' victory against the Minnesota Vikings referred incompletely in some editions to the Falcons' owner. He is Arthur Blank.

Sports: The TV listings Nov. 25 misidentified the college basketball tournament final in which Stanford and Syracuse were to meet. It was the Dick's Sporting Goods NIT Season Tip-Off, which was won by Syracuse.

News: A story Nov. 22 on the Republican presidential candidates misstated al-Qaeda leader Osama bin Laden's first name.

Life: Star Wars: The Old Republic: Revan is No. 45 on USA TODAY's Best-Selling Books list. Part of the subtitle was misspelled in the paper Nov. 23.

Money: A story Nov. 22 on Chevrolet's Spark small car should have listed the engine displacement as 1.2 liters.

Sports: The listing Nov. 22 of Top 25 women's basketball scores included an incorrect result in some editions. No. 4 Connecticut defeated No. 5 Stanford 68-58.

Life: A story Nov. 17 about social drinking cited Gallup data for 2010 but omitted 2011 data. Gallup says 64% of Americans consumed alcohol in 2011. Also, the percentage who abstain was wrong in the graphic; it is 35%, says the National Institute on Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism.

News: A story Nov. 16 about the growth of land being placed into land trusts misstated an explanation of tax benefits. The tax deduction comes from donating development rights.

News: Some editions Nov. 16 containing a 1A story on the wealthiest members of Congress omitted the fifth wealthiest member, Sen. Herbert Kohl, D-Wis., a former owner of a chain of grocery and department stores. Some editions misstated his net worth, which is estimated at $173 million. The incorrect information was provided by the Center for Responsive Politics. Kohl's addition increases the number of Senate Democrats in the top 1% of net worth to 13 and the number of millionaires in Congress to 250.

Sports: A story Nov. 15 about the Minnesota-Green Bay Monday Night Football game misstated in some editions the number of games between the two teams that did not involve quarterback Brett Favre since he joined Packers in 1992. There have been four (Favre played 32 as a Packer and four as a Viking).

Sports: A news brief Nov. 14 about a high school football game involving a USA TODAY Super 25 team referred incorrectly to the outcome. Then-No. 12 Central Catholic (Pittsburgh) defeated Mount Lebanon 31-14.

Sports: A story Nov. 14 about Kasey Kahne's NASCAR victory Nov. 13 in Arizona referred incompletely to his driving history with Ford. He drove for both Robert Yates Racing and Richard Petty Motorsports.

Sports: A headline Nov. 7 with a story about the Green Bay Packers' 45-38 victory against the San Diego Chargers referred incorrectly to the scoring sequence of the game. Although the Packers were ahead by 21 points at one time, they did not lead 21-0.

Life: Pediatrician Robert Shapiro of Cincinnati Children's Hospital Medical Center was incorrectly identified in a story Nov. 10 about recognizing signs of child sexual abuse.

News: At the Republican presidential debate in Michigan Nov. 9, former Massachusetts governor Mitt Romney suggested the Environmental Protection Agency when Texas Gov. Rick Perry couldn't remember the name of the federal agency he had proposed eliminating. A story Nov. 10 misidentified the speaker.

News: A 1A headline in some editions Nov. 10 mischaracterized who was jeered during the GOP debate. Members of the audience booed a questioner who asked Herman Cain about sexual harassment accusations.

News: A photo caption identifying U.S. Appeals Court Judge Laurence Silberman was omitted Nov. 9 from a 1A story on the federal health care law.

News: The caption on a photo Oct. 7 of Mitt Romney aboard the USS Yorktown mischaracterized the ship. It is an aircraft carrier.

Sports: A story Nov. 3 previewing a Nov. 5 bout between Lucian Bute and Glen Johnson referred incompletely to one of Bute's victories. Five years ago he defeated James Obede Toney, not James "Lights Out" Toney.

Sports: An item in the Sports on TV column Nov. 2 about matchups of No. 1 vs. No. 2 in college football referred incorrectly in some editions to the winner of the most recent regular-season showdown. Ohio State defeated Michigan 42-39 in 2006.

News: The last name of the family featured in a story Nov. 1 about tornado warnings was incorrect in two references. The correct family name is Schaffer.

Sports: A story Nov. 1 about Kim Kardashian filing for divorce from Kris Humphries referred imprecisely to his status as an NBA player. Although Humphries was with the New Jersey Nets last season, he is now a free agent.

Life: A story Nov. 2 about wine misstated where Left Foot Charley Pinot Blanc is grown. It is grown on Old Mission Peninsula in Michigan.

News: In some editions Nov. 1, a 1A story on Afghan security forces misstated the years for certain costs and the projected size of the forces. The cost to train and equip them was $11.6billion in 2011. A Pentagon report said Afghan forces and police will meet their required number by October 2012.

News: A story Nov. 1 about a rare October snowstorm misstated the source who said the USA has had a record 12 natural disasters that each caused $1 billion in damage this year. It was Steve Bowen, a meteorologist at AON Corp.

Sports: A story Oct. 31 about Game 7 of the World Series referred incorrectly to the number of seasons the Texas Rangers franchise has gone without a World Series title. The streak is now at 40 years.

Sports: The listing of golf results Oct. 31 omitted the winner of the European Andalucia Masters. Sergio Garcia won with a 6-under-par 278.

News: A story Oct. 31 about Department of Energy loans misstated which Westinghouse Electric facilities are in the district of Rep. Tim Murphy, R-Pa. They are as service center and a manufacturing plant. Company headquarters are outside the district.

News: A 1A story Oct. 24 about states curbing hospital stays under Medicaid omitted exemptions to Hawaii's planned 10-day limit. It exempts children, pregnant women, those undergoing cancer treatment, the elderly, blind and disabled.

News: A story Oct. 28 on the USS Iowa misstated its size. It was the biggest U.S. battleship; two Japanese battleships sunk by U.S. forces in World War II were larger and had bigger guns.

Money: In the Investing column Oct. 28, a recommendation for stock in automaker Daimler should have been attributed to Oakmark fund manager Ron Taylor, not Templeton manager Norm Boersma.

Life: A photo caption Oct. 26 on page 4D for the prime-time Nielsen ratings incorrectly identified New York Jets running back Shonn Greene.

News: A story Oct. 19 on student loans exceeding credit card debt omitted sources for that information. The credit card data were from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York and the student loan data from the U.S. Department of Education and private sources.

Sports: A photo caption Oct. 24 with a story about Albert Pujols' exceptional performance in Game 3 of the World Series included an incorrect batting line in some editions. The St. Louis Cardinals first baseman was 5-for-6.

Money: A story Oct. 24 about video game Battlefield 3 misidentified the network to which Electronic Arts CEO John Riccitiello spoke. It was Fox Business Network.

Money: In the Personal Technology column Oct. 20 on ultrabook computers, certain battery information was mislabeled. The Asus Zenbook got about 3¼ hours of battery life in USA TODAY tests. And the Zenbook is the notebook with a handy onscreen battery gauge and the model that automatically saves files when the battery life drops below 5%.

Life: A story Oct. 20 about actor Kelsey Grammer incorrectly identified one of his short-lived sitcoms. It was Back to You.

Sports: A story Oct. 20 about boxer Nonito Donaire referred incorrectly to the weight limit for his last bantamweight fight. It was 118 pounds. He will move up to 122 pounds for his next fight.

Sports: A chart Oct. 19 of quarterbacks who are Heisman Trophy contenders transposed the passing attempts and completions in the previous week's games for each of the five players. A corrected version of the chart appeared on Page 11C Oct. 20.

Sports: A news brief and chart Oct. 17 about the seven NFL teams that sought to defend their titles after beginning the season 6-0 omitted a team that followed up with a championship. The 2004 New England Patriots repeated as champs after a 6-0 start, joining the 1998 Denver Broncos as the only teams to do so.

Sports: A story Oct. 18 examining postseason baseball facts and myths referred incorrectly to the record of National League teams playing at home in the World Series since 2000. It is 18-12.

Money: A story Oct. 17 incorrectly identified Dr. Steven Nissen of the Cleveland Clinic as a surgeon. He is a cardiologist.

News: A story Oct. 18 on local government job cuts misstated the size of Chicago's budget deficit. It is $636 million.

News: A story Oct. 7 on new strategies in teaching and homework incorrectly identified Lisa Nielsen's new book. It is Teaching Generation Text.

News: A story Oct. 10 about cuts in military funds for eye trauma research misstated the name of the Blinded Veterans Association.

News: DeAnza College student Anirudh Chava's name was misspelled in a story Oct. 7 about Steve Jobs.

News: A story Oct. 11 on San Antonio's water conservation misstated how much water residents use. The average is about 130 gallons per person a day.

Sports: A story Oct. 6 about St. Louis Cardinals third baseman David Freese referred imprecisely to his collegiate baseball career. He played one season at Meramec (Mo.) Community College and a second at the University of South Alabama.

Life: Jeff Hirsch is the author of The Eleventh Plague. His name was misspelled in a review Oct. 6.

Money: A story Oct. 4 on the future of the U.S. solar industry misstated the status of a factory First Solar is building in Mesa, Ariz. The factory is not yet open, but the solar manufacturing company expects to open it for production next year.

Sports: A photo caption Oct. 3 with a story about Major League Baseball playoffs incorrectly described the final out of the Detroit Tigers-New York Yankees game in some editions. Yankees second baseman Robinson Cano grounded out.

Life: In some editions Oct. 3, a report about Cirque du Soleil's Michael Jackson tribute, The Immortal World Tour, misidentified one of the Jackson brothers. Tito, Jackie and Marlon Jackson attended the premiere in Montreal.

Money: A story Sept. 30 on Research In Motion's PlayBook tablet computer should have made clear that NPD Group's DisplaySearch analyst Richard Shim was referring to supply-chain research that indicates Quanta, not RIM, doesn't have production plans for PlayBooks beyond this year.

Life: Henry Holt is the publisher of Killing Lincoln: The Shocking Assassination That Changed America Forever by Bill O'Reilly and Martin Dugard. Scribner was incorrectly listed as one of the publishers in a story Sept. 29.

Opinion: A Sept. 29 column by Laura Vanderkam about young voters should have said that a survey of Millennials last year was done by the Pew Research Center.

News: The pie chart on a Sept. 27 1A Snapshot was mislabeled. The 45% of respondents who said people have the same willingness to help others as 10 years ago should have been represented by the lower segment of the pie chart.

Opinion: A list with Sept. 27's editorial on immigration should have noted that a Maryland law, which allows undocumented immigrants to pay in-state tuition at public colleges, has been suspended pending the outcome of a referendum in November 2012.

Life: In a story Sept. 27 about knees, the first name of the president of the National Athletic Trainers' Association was misspelled. Her name is Marje Albohm.

Sports: The Los Angeles Dodgers team note Sept. 27 omitted two points when putting Clayton Kershaw's season into historical context. The left-hander is the fourth pitcher since 1970 to win at least 20 games and strike out more than 240 in a season while under the age of 24.

Sports: A story Sept. 26 about The Tour Championship included a misspelled surname in some editions for the winner. He is Bill Haas.

Sports: A news brief Sept. 26 about the historical dominance of the Indianapolis Colts and Tennessee Titans misidentified their division in some editions. It is the AFC South.

News: The special report Sept. 23 about state legislators' pensions misidentified the party affiliation of Nebraska state Sen. Jeremy Nordquist. He is a Democrat. A related story gave an incorrect number for retirees in the Oregon state pension system. There are 110,000.

News: A story in some editions Sept. 23 about a helium shortage incorrectly attributed a prediction that improvement was expected in the fourth quarter. The statement came from Helen Carmichael, editor of trade journal Specialty Gas Report.

News: A story Sept. 22 on the disaster at Japan's Fukushima Dai-ichi nuclear plant should have made clear the burning at the spent fuel pools involved hydrogen explosions and did not burn the spent fuel rods themselves.

Sports: A photo caption Sept. 20 with a story about St. Louis Cardinals Hall of Famer Stan Musial included a misspelled given name for a fellow Hall of Fame member. He is Willie Mays.

News: A story Sept. 20 on East Jerusalem misstated its demographics. It is 57% Arab, according to a report of the Jerusalem Institute for Israel Studies.

Money: A chart accompanying a story Sept. 20 on how airlines continue to raise fees should have said Hawaiian Airlines charges $100 to check a bag 63 by 80 inches between Hawaii and the mainland, $80 to $125 between Hawaii and international destinations and $25 within Hawaii.

Life: In some editions of the Sept. 19 Life section, the winner of the variety, musical or comedy series Emmy was misidentified. It was Comedy Central's Daily Show With Jon Stewart.

News: In a story and headline Sept. 14 on an independent Palestinian state, Palestinian Ambassador Maen Areikat says he was referring to Israelis, not Jews, when he stated that "it would be in the best interest of the two people to be separated first."

News: An item in Across the USA on Sept. 15 should have said the University of Virginia is located in Charlottesville.

Life: In a story Sept. 15 about eyeglass fashion, two frame styles were mislabeled. The example of "Vintage revivals" was Phillip Lim Sabine by MODO; the Geek chic" example was Kensie Hipster by Kenmark Group.

Money: A story that ran Aug. 30 should have noted that Aug. 29 was the deadline set by the Interactive Advertising Bureau, a trade association for 500 ad-supported U.S.-based digital companies, for all its members to be compliant with a new code of conduct regarding privacy.

Sports: Sagarin college football rankings from last season were mistakenly included in some editions on Sept. 13. Up-to-date rankings can be found at sagarin.usatoday.com.

Sports: The college football scores and schedules on Sept. 13 included incorrect entries for two schools. Western Michigan lost to Michigan 34-10 and defeated Nicholls State 38-17; Mississippi State defeated Memphis 59-14 and is 1-1.

Life: A story Sept. 12 on low-vision therapy did not fully identify the location of the University of Alabama-Birmingham Center for Low Vision Rehabilitation.

Money: A story Sept. 12 about cable customers switching to Web-based services should have attributed information on the number of U.S. homes with traditional TVs to Nielsen.

News: A headline in the Sept. 9 paper should have said that illicit drugs are currently used by 8.9% of the U.S. population 12 years and older.

Life: A puppet show by Dan Hurlin is called Hiroshima Maiden. The title was incorrect in a story Sept. 1 about Brian Selznick.

News: A story Sept. 6 on federal regulations misstated Boeing's intention for an aircraft plant in South Carolina. The aircraft manufacturer wishes to build its 787 Dreamliner at a nonunion assembly plant in South Carolina.

Money: The source of an illustration with a story Sept. 1 about space debris should have been identified as The Aerospace Corp.

Money: A story Sept. 2 about 3-D TV misidentified the Olympics that will be held in London in 2012. They are the Summer Games.

News: A story Aug. 29 about a shorebird that flew through Hurricane Irene misstated the species' breeding grounds. Whimbrels breed in the Arctic.

Sports: A story Aug. 25 about Street League Skateboarding misstated the amount Nyjah Huston will receive if he wins the final Aug. 28 in Newark. The top prize is $200,000. Huston has an estimated $800,000 in career prize money and could become the first skateboarder to reach $1 million.

Life: A story Aug. 23 misstated the name of the book An Unbroken Bond: The Untold Story of How the Cantor Fitzgerald Families Faced the Tragedy of 9/11 and Beyond.

Sports: A news brief headline Aug. 22 misidentified the publisher of the NBA 2K12 video game. It is 2K Sports.

News: The victim of an alleged extortion plot in Australia involving a fake bomb was misidentified in some editions Aug. 17. Her name is Madeleine Pulver.

Life: A story Aug. 15 misstated the name of sports medicine doctor Carol Otis.

Life: A photo caption Aug. 18 misidentified the store where Kim Kardashian and Kris Humphries were shopping. They were at Scott Hill Bespoke Designs in Beverly Hills.

Opinion: The opposing view in the debate Aug. 15 on recall elections should have said that the Milwaukee county executive resigned amid the threat of recall in 2002, not that he was recalled.

Life: A story Aug. 15 about country singer Luke Bryan gave an incorrect location of the stage collapse that killed five people Aug. 13 at a Sugarland concert. The show was at the Indiana State Fairgrounds.

Sports: A Spectator Sports column Aug. 12 about NFL on-air lineups included a misspelled given name and incorrect playing position for an NFL Network analyst in some editions. He is former quarterback Kurt Warner.

Sports: A news brief and headline Aug. 11 about two Earnhardt Ganassi Racing employees who were fired after being arrested on drug charges referred incorrectly to their working arrangement. Although they also were suspended by NASCAR, the men were not NASCAR employees.

News: A map of the USA accompanying a 1A cover story about the nation's demographic changes did not make clear that percentages shown in four regions represent their population growth since 1990: 36% in the West, 34% in the South, 12% in the Midwest and 9% in the Northeast.

Sports: A story Aug. 9 about Dan Uggla of the Atlanta Braves extending his hitting streak to 29 games the previous night misidentified the player who has the longest streak in franchise history. Tommy Holmes hit in 37 consecutive games in 1945.

Sports: A Snapshot Aug. 9 showing players with the most touchdown passes in a single ArenaBowl misidentified Matt D'Orazio's team when he had seven TD tosses. It was the Philadelphia Soul.

Sports: A story Aug. 5 about the Oklahoma-Oklahoma State athletic rivalry mischaracterized the schools' performance in men's basketball. Oklahoma leads the series 127-93.

Sports: A list Aug. 5 of major-college football schedules omitted a game on the Missouri schedule. It hosts Texas on Nov. 12.

Life: Tiffany Wingate of Florence, Miss., whose daughter starts college this fall, is a nurse practitioner. Her job was incorrect in Aug. 2's Parenting Part II column.

News: A story Aug. 4 about an exhibit on last year's rescue of Chilean miners misstated the exhibit's location. It is on display at the National Museum of Natural History in Washington, D.C.

News: A story Aug. 3 on ROTC returning to campuses misstated the vote tally to allow the program?s return to Columbia University. It was 51-17.

News: Aug. 3's Weather Page had the incorrect extreme low temperature. It was 36 degrees at Noatak, Alaska.

Sports: An item in Aug. 3's Keeping Score column about the NBA lockout referred incorrectly to the league's performance in providing information to the players union. The NBA has shared audited financial data and tax returns through the 2009-10 season and audited basketball-related income for 2010-11. The union does not dispute that the league has opened its books but questions the interpretation of the numbers.

Sports: The National League roundup Aug. 3 referred incorrectly in some editions to the team in the Philadelphia-Colorado matchup that was seeking its fifth consecutive victory. It was the Phillies, who got the win.

Opinion: Barack Obama is the third president of this millennium. A Forum column Aug. 2 incorrectly said he was the second.

Life: Just One Catch: A Biography of Joseph Heller is the title of a new book by Tracy Daugherty. The title was incorrect in a story Aug. 1.

Life: In a story July 29 on 51 great places to watch wildlife, the Piedras Blancas elephant seal rookery near San Simeon, Calif., was misidentified as Año Nuevo State Park.

Sports: A news brief Aug. 1 about the under-19 women's world basketball championships misidentified the site of the event. It was Puerto Montt, Chile.

Sports: A promotional item July 29 keying to a Page 1A cover story about a sports memorabilia collector who was killed when a plane crashed into his home misidentified the type of aircraft that was involved. It was a turboprop.

Life: Keli Goff, author of The GQ Candidate, was an intern for Hillary Clinton's New York Senate campaign. That information was incorrect in a story July 28.

News: A story July 27 about the resignation of Rep. David Wu, D-Ore., misstated his place in congressional history. He is the first Chinese American elected to the U.S. House of Representatives.

Life: Stephany Flores was murdered in Peru on May 30, 2010. The date was incorrect in a review July 21 of the book Portrait of A Monster: Joran van der Sloot, a Murder in Peru and the Natalee Holloway Mystery.

Life: Natalee Holloway, who disappeared in Aruba in 2005, was from Mountain Brook, Ala. Her home state was incorrect in a story July 21

Life: In early editions July 21, a quote about TV seriesCharlie's Angels was attributed incorrectly. Sony Pictures Television's Jamie Erlicht said, "It wasn't a grab for cash, or 'Let's get back into our library and find something a network will be excited about.'"

Life: Paul Feig directed Bridesmaids. An item in a July 20 Lifeline column mistakenly identified another director.

News: A story July 14 about Betty Ford's funeral in Michigan misstated when Gerald and Betty Ford were married. It was Oct. 15, 1948.

News: A July 20 1A story on the U.S. Postal Service misstated the position of Sen. Susan Collins, R-Maine, on ending Saturday mail delivery. She opposes the proposal.

News: A headline in a July 19 paper for a story on gauges used to assess brain injuries among U.S. troops should have made clear that the devices were developed by the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency.

News: Twenty-eight percent of DNA exonerations nationally have involved defendents who pleaded guilty, falsely confessed or made incriminating statements to police. A story July 18 included only those who pleaded guilty in that percentage.

News: A story July 15 about falling crime rates in Arizona border cities did not specify which cities were analyzed. Violent offenses such as armed robberies and homicides have fallen from 1998 to 2009 in cities and towns within 100 miles of the border.

News: A story July 13 about first lady Betty Ford's funeral in Palm Desert, Calif., was unclear about her late husband's unique status. Gerald Ford was the only vice president and president who was not elected to either position. The story also did not make clear when he died. It was in 2006.

Money: A July 6 story about foreign buyers of U.S. homes should have said the number of U.S. homes sold to foreigners dropped to 224,000 in the 12 months ended in March, from 288,000 a year earlier, according to a survey by the National Association of Realtors.

Sports: A photo July 14 with a story about the Viking Classic was misidentified as Heath Slocum. The golfer is Russell Henley.

News: In some editions July 13, a 1A photo of a memorial service for Betty Ford misspelled the photographer's name. It was taken by David Hume Kennerly, AP. Also, a 1A photo showing Medal of Honor recipent Army Sgt. 1st Class Leroy Petry had an incorrect credit. It was taken by Garrett Hubbard, USA TODAY.

Opinion: In a letter published July 13, reader Aaron Rochlen's name was incorrect.

Life: Green Lantern has earned $109.7 million at the box office. Mr. Popper's Penguins has grossed $58.1 million. Incorrect totals for the films were listed in a July 12 box-office chart.

News: In some editions July 12, a caption with a 1A photo of British Prime Minister David Cameron misstated his connection to the British phone-hacking scandal. There are no current allegations that his family's phone messages were hacked.

News: A July 8 1A story on back-to-school marketing misidentified a sale item at Staples. It is a two-tone notebook.

News: The caption on a 1A photo July 11 of a Red Sox player misidentified him. He is J.D. Drew. The incorrect information was provided by Getty.

Sports: The All-Star Game rosters July 12 included an incorrect affiliation for Curtis Granderson in some editions. He is an outfielder for the New York Yankees.

Sports: A news brief July 13 about former NBA player Lance Allred included an incorrect address for his website. It is LanceAllred41.com.

Life: Diana Gabaldon's Outlander series will continue after An Echo in the Bone. The status of the series was incorrect in a story July 7.

Sports: A cover story July 6 about Washington Nationals prospect Bryce Harper attributed a quote to the wrong speaker. The quote, describing how fans sometimes greet Harper, was said by Hagerstown (Md.) Suns pitcher Taylor Jordan.

News: A cover story July 8 about Derek Jeter's place in New York Yankees history misspelled the name of a New York University professor. It is Wayne McDonnell.
