News: A photo caption with a story July 6 about 9/11 memorials misidentified the highway motorcycle escorts were traveling between Richmond, Ind., and Indianapolis. It is Interstate 70.

Sports: A story July 7 about the top players in Oakland Athletics franchise history included an incorrect career home run total for Jimmie Foxx. The Hall of Famer had 534 home runs.

Sports: A news brief July 6 about increases in ATP prize money referred incorrectly to the rate at which purses will go up for all World Tour Masters 1000 tournaments. They will increase 9% each of the next three years.

Sports: A breakdown July 6 of the cities vying for the 2018 Winter Olympics omitted a previous Olympics that had been held in Germany. Berlin hosted the 1936 Summer Games.

Sports: A story July 6 about golfer Yani Tseng referred incorrectly in one instance to her nationality. She is Taiwanese.

Sports: A chart July 5 listing players named to baseball's All-Star Game omitted the catchers for the National League squad. They are Brian McCann of the Atlanta Braves and Yadier Molina of the St. Louis Cardinals.

News: Since he did so in 1976, only Gerald Ford has won a contested Iowa Republican caucuses and carried the state in the November general election. A story July 5 on the Iowa caucuses misstated his accomplishment that year.

Money: A timeline showing events from the past 15 years at Hewlett-Packard published June 30 included incorrect images for the company co-founders.

Money: A story June 30 about the sale of Myspace contained a math error. Facebook's estimated value is nearly 3,000 times higher than the $35 million sale price of Myspace.

News: A chart accompanying a June 30 1A story on college costs did not make clear that tuition includes room and board charges at some colleges.

News: A story June 28 on an Afghanistan offensive misidentified the location of a U.S. outpost overrun in 2009. It was in Nuristan province.

News: A graphic June 28 on the final launch of the space shuttle incorrectly listed possible space shuttle landing sites. Only Florida and California are possible landing sites.

News: In some editions June 28, a front-page item about Beyonce's new album should have said the review was on page 7B.

Money: A story June 29 overstated what AOL paid for Rally Up; the amount was less than $10 million.

Money: A story June 29 about universities turning to outsourced instructors misstated the percentage of Lamar University's total enrollment in two master's in education programs. The enrollment of 4,100 was more than 20% of the total.

Life: A story on kids' programming June 30 gave an incorrect return date for Nickelodeon's Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles. It will premiere in fall 2012.

Sports: A cover story June 28 about college athletics finances referred incorrectly to an NCAA classroom-performance measurement. It is the Academic Progress Rate.

Sports: A news brief June 28 about Wimbledon TV ratings included an incorrect given name for the only U.S. men?s singles player left in the field at that time. He is Mardy Fish.

Sports: A listing June 29 of candidates for the inaugural High School Sullivan Award included an incorrect surname for a student-athlete from Libertyville (Ill.) High. He is Teddy Kelley.

Sports: A story published June 29 about the book Ron Santo: A Perfect 10 had an incorrect list price. It is $24.95.

Money: The June 13 Cars.com/USA TODAY comparison of five compact sedans should have listed the Honda Civic in second place and the Chevrolet Cruze in third place. A data error by Cars.com reversed their positions. Rankings of the other vehicles did not change.

Money: A Test Drive feature June 17 should have said the Chrysler 300 has a navigation system by Garmin that is displayed on a screen supplied by Alpine.

Money: A story June 22 about NBA rookie Blake Griffin being named spokesman for the Kia auto brand misstated the first name of David Carter, principal of the Sports Business Group.

Sports: The sourceline for tables that accompanied a story June 16 about college athletics finances omitted the participation of students from Indiana University's National Sports Journalism Center, led by center director Timothy A. Franklin. The students assisted in acquiring documents from the schools and inputting data from the documents.

Sports: The Major League Baseball statistics package June 14 omitted data from the Seattle Mariners.

News: A front page story June 15 on Republican presidential candidate Michele Bachmann misstated the organization founded by Judson Phillips. It is the Tea Party Nation.

Money: A story June 14 misstated the number of solar panel leases expected to be sold in 2011 by the company Sungevity of Oakland. The company expects to do 3,000 leases this year, up from 1,000 last year.

Sports: A subhead June 13 with a news brief about Cael Sanderson qualifying for a world championship event in September in Istanbul misidentified the sport in which he was competing. It is wrestling.

Sports: A photo caption June 13 with a story about a football coaching change at West Virginia misidentified the person who was pictured most prominently. He is coach Dana Holgorsen, who was joined in the background by athletics director Oliver Luck.

Sports: A photo caption June 13 with a story about the Belmont Stakes imprecisely described Kentucky Derby winner Animal Kingdom's start in the race. Shortly after leaving the starting gate, he bumped hard with Mucho Macho Man, causing jockey John Velazquez to briefly lose his left iron.

Life: June 13's coverage of the Bonnaroo Music & Arts Festival incorrectly identified the lineup of the original Meters. Art Neville is the band's organist; Ivan Neville is his nephew.

News: The credit for a photo of Kevin Knapp accompanying a story June 10 on a Civil War hot-air balloon re-enactment was incorrect. The photo was taken by John Michael.

Opinion: When companies check the credit histories of people applying for jobs, they are not given the applicants' credit scores but rather a more limited report tailored for use by prospective employers. An editorial June 6 described the process inaccurately.

Sports: A results chart June 13 from the first race on Saturday's Belmont Park racing card was mistakenly labeled as the chart for the Belmont Stakes.

Money: Duncan Mac Naughton is chief merchandising officer at Walmart. His title was incorrect in a story June 10.

Life: Actress Kristen Stewart is 21. Her age was incorrect in a cover story June 8.

News: The taxpayer liability for federal financial obligations that are not paid for totals $528,000 per U.S. household. The $2 trillion obligation for federal employee retirement benefits amounts to $17,000 per household. A story June 7 on the liability gave incorrect household figures.

News: A June 7 1A cover story about Rep. Anthony Weiner misstated the Census being used for redistricting. It is the 2010 Census.

News: A June 7 cover story about Rep. Anthony Weiner misstated the Census being used for redistricting. It is the 2010 Census.

Sports: A story June 6 about the draft potential of Oklahoma high school pitcher Dylan Bundy misstated how his Owasso team fared in this year?s Class 6A state championship. It lost to Broken Arrow High.

News: An "Across the USA" item June 6 about Los Angeles teachers approving a tentative agreement with the school board should have been datelined Los Angeles.

Life: The title of Transformers: Dark of the Moon was incorrect in a movie roundup June 3.

News: A caption with a photo June 3 of Marines learning agriculture at California State University-Fresno, misidentified the instructor. He is crop adviser Paul Sommers.

News: A story June 2 on Republican presidential candidate Mitt Romney failed to note that Rep. Ron Paul also previously ran for the GOP nomination in 2008.

Opinion: Because of an editing error, a June 1 editorial on college football failed to fully identify Ohio State President E. Gordon Gee.

Sports: A story June 1 about Little League's Urban Initiative misstated how the program is funded. It is a unit of Little League International. The story also referred incorrectly to a donation from Dick's Sporting Goods. It was for several gift items, not baseball equipment. Also the age range for players invited to the weekend jamboree in Richmond, Va., was 9-12.

News: A story June 1 about reduced premiums in the federal pre-existing condition insurance program misstated the average monthly payment in Florida for a health savings account plan for a person older than 55. It will drop 40% to $390 a month.

News: In a story May 27 about pets rescued after the Joplin, Mo., tornado, one of the two national organizations was misidentified. It is the American Humane Association.

Life: A caption May 27, with a photo of the new FX series Wilfrid, misspelled the name of one of the actors. Her name is Fiona Gubelmann.

News: A story May 26 about a special congressional election in New York misstated the 2012 election forecast made by Rep. Steve Israel, chairman of the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee. He said there is an analysis that 97 House districts are more moderate than New York's 26th District and could expand the party's playing field for the 2012 elections.

News: A story May 23 on President Obama's speech on Israel-Palestinian peace talks misstated the locationof the armistice line betwween Israel and the West Bank. It is along eastern Israel.

Life: Geoffrey Gray's forthcoming non-fiction book is called Skyjack: The Hunt for D.B. Cooper. The subtitle was incorrect in the May 26 paper.

Sports: A story May 24 about Chicago Bull center Joakim Noah's fine for shouting an anti-gay slur at a fan during a game against the Miami Heat misstated in some editions where the fan was seated. He was behind the Bulls bench.

Sports: A story May 24 about an exchange between Miami Heat fans and TNT's Charles Barkley after a May 22 game with the Chicago Bulls omitted Barkley's response as the fans' expletive-laced chant continued. He raised his arms and made obscene gestures with both hands.

News: A story May 23 on states promoting safer biking contained an incorrect affiliation for Ethan Spotts. He is spokesman for the Chicago-based Active Transportation Alliance.

Sports: A story May 23 about the Indianapolis 500 field misidentified the car Simona de Silvestro, who qualified 24th May 21, was driving when she wrecked two days earlier. It was the No. 78 Honda.

News: A May 20 weather focus omitted the label "2011 hurricane forecast" that identified the bars in the graphic.

Life: USA TODAY's Carol Memmott wrote a May 19 review of The Snowman by Jo Nesbo. Her byline was missing.

News: A May 10 story about hunger in the USA misstated how many people are enrolled in a major federal nutrition program. About one-third of people who qualify for the program are not enrolled to receive benefits, according to the Agriculture Department.

Money: The publication Nation's Restaurant News, which was referred to in a story May 17 about restaurant price inflation, is owned by Penton Media and is not affiliated with a trade group.

Sports: A story May 19 about IndyCar driver Charlie Kimball, who has diabetes, referred incorrectly to a particular insulin-delivery device. It is called a FlexPen.

Life: The location of author Bill James was incorrect in a photo caption May 17. He was in Madison Square park in Manhattan.

Life: The name of actor Josh Holloway's daugther was incorrect in a story May 12. Her name is Java Kumala Holloway.

News: A May 12 1A cover story on the effects of dust on troops in Iraq and Afghanistan misstated the impact of bacteria in the dust on cystic fibrosis.They can worsen the disease, a genetic disorder.

News: A 1A newspaper story May 10 about uninsured Americans misstated the ability of uninsured families to pay their hospital costs. It should have said that uninsured families can afford to pay 12% of their hospital bills in full. It also should have said that the estimate of $49 billion a year in unpaid hospital bills came from USA TODAY's analysis of government statistics.

News: A story May 6 on congressional budget talks misidentified the role of U.S. Rep. Chris Van Hollen, D-Md. Van Hollen is the ranking Democrat on the House Budget Committee.

News: In some editions May 6, Diane Wall and her daughter Payton were misidentified in a story and photo caption about President Obama visiting Ground Zero and meeting with families of 9/11 victims.

Opinion: A May 6 opposing view on Afghanistan by Rep. Barbara Lee misstated the cost of the war. It is about $100 billion a year, not $100 billion a month.

Money: Wikipedia co-founder Jimmy Wales denies that he ever broke up with a girlfriend on Wikipedia, as reported in a story May 11.

Money: A story May 4 should have said Alissa White of Matcha Source gets her teas from Japan.

Money: The Test Drive column May 6 should have said the Lexus CT 200h hybrid sedan is the fifth best-selling Lexus, behind the RX crossover SUV, ES sedan, IS sedan and GX SUV.

Opinion: A May 4 editorial on taxes misstated the percentages of income attributable to upper-income individuals. The top 1% earn about 20% of the nation's household income; the top 10% about 46%. The editorial also should have said that since the 1990s the top 1% have accounted for more than 30% of the nation's wealth, not the nation's income.

Sports: An Open Mikes column May 6 about Indianapolis 500 pace-car drivers misidentified Chuck Yeager's profession when he served in that role. He was a test pilot.

Sports: A photo caption May 5 with a cover story about boxer Manny Pacquiao misidentified in some editions the location of the suite where he was pictured. It was at Mandalay Bay in Las Vegas.

Life: May 4 prime-time Nielsen charts carried the wrong dates. They were for the week of April 25-May1.

News: A photo caption for an April 8 cover story on descendants of key Civil War figures incorrectly described the relationship of Henry Shaffner of Philadelphia to Confederate Gen. Stonewall Jackson. Shaffner is Jackson's great-great-grandson.

Sports: A compilation in a May 3 edition of Tweets from athletes and sports personalities reacting to Osama bin Laden's death incorrectly stated the origin of comments made by Mariano Rivera. The New York Yankees reliever's comment was made verbally, not tweeted.

News: The Web address for the organization Our Time was incorrect in a May 4 cover story on young people's reactions to Osama bin Laden's death. The correct address is www.ourtime.org.

News: Dollar figures in an April 25 article about state job creation programs represent the budgets for the states' economic development agencies, not the states' total spending on such efforts.

Sports: A Keeping Score item on May 2 incorrectly attributed tweets to a Twitter account of Hall of Fame basketball player Bill Walton. They actually came from a parody Twitter account.

Life: Bharati Mukherjee's new novel, to be published in May, is Miss New India. The title was incorrect in a story April 28.

News: The name of Wisconsin resident Steve Jozefczyk was misspelled in one reference in an April 29 story on town-hall-style meetings held by U.S. Rep. Paul Ryan.

Sports: A chart April 27 comparing NBA cities New Orleans and Memphis referred incompletely to the burial of Jefferson Davis, president of the Confederacy. Although he was temporarily buried in New Orleans, his final resting place is Richmond.

Life: A story April 15 misspelled the name of NASCAR Sprint Cup Series race car driver Jimmie Johnson.

News: The first name of Marlene Roll, organizer of a VFW post for women in the Buffalo area, was incorrect in some editions April 25.

Life: A story April 19 on green cities misstated the percentage of America's energy consumed by industrial and commercial buildings in 2009. The correct number is 42%.

Sports: A special package April 21 about the All-USA high school boys basketball team included an incorrect record for No.l St. Anthony (Jersey City), led by coach of the year Bob Hurley. The Friars were 33-0.

News: A story April 21 about Amish conflicts with local laws misidentified the town that the Becket Fund for Religious Liberty sued. It is Morristown, N.Y.

News: In a story about online searches for Donald Trump in some editions April 21, the wrong location was given for Northeastern University. It is in Boston.

Money: BMW says its electric-power i3 sedan coming in 2013 is a new design that's unrelated to the current 3-series gasoline-power cars. A story in the April 19 newspaper was incorrect.

Sports: A chart with an April 13 cover story about concussions in pro sports referred imprecisely to the NHL policy in two instances. During a game, there is no league-mandated time a player must be evaluated before he can re-enter the contest. Also, if a concussion has been diagnosed, there is no specific length of time a skater must be held out of play.

Sports: A video posted April 6 in connection with a story about sprinter Usain Bolt should have included a photo of Canadian sprinter Ben Johnson. Instead, a photo of another runner was inadvertently used. The video has been corrected.

Life: The location of the Ponca Powwow is in Ponca City, Okla. The city was incorrect in a story April 15.

Life: In a Passover story April 18, the date for the webcam/live chat Online Seder by OurJewish Community.org was incorrect. The event is 5 p.m. ET April 19.

News: A story April 15 about cities considering golf carts for use on city streets misstated their cost. The difference in price between a golf cart and a low-speed vehicle ranges from $500 to $2,000.

Life: A story April 15 misstated the film studio releasing John Carter of Mars. It is Disney.

News: In a story April 14 about former senator Rick Santorum exploring a run for the Republican presidential nomination, a photo caption mischaracterized his position on the role of moral issues in the campaign. Santorum said America "is a moral enterprise at its core."

Money: A story published to the website April 13, based on a report from the Associated Press saying that GE would repay a tax refund, has been removed. The source of that story was a press release that proved to be a hoax.

Sports: A chart April 14 showing how the jury voted on each of the four charges against Barry Bonds misstated in some editions the breakdown on one count. Jurors voted 11-1 to convict on the second perjury count, under which Bonds was charged with lying about only getting injected by a doctor.

Life: The name of a founder of mobile game company Chillingo as misstated in a reference in a story April 12. His name is Joe Wee.

Money: A story April 7 should have said small businesses seeking working capital for exports can get a Small Business Administration guarantee for up to a $5 million loan, up from $2 million previously.

Sports: The NBA standings April 11 included incorrect records for two teams. Entering that night's games, Charlotte was 32-48 and Golden State 35-45.

Sports: A story April 11 about Minnesota-Duluth's NCAA men's hockey championship misidentified the school for which UMD coach Scott Sandelin played in the 1984 Frozen Four. He was a defenseman for North Dakota.

Money: A list April 5 about the hack of marketing firm Epsilon included two companies, Visa and Borders, that say they were not affected by the database breach.

Life: An interview March 28 with Toni Collette incorrectly stated the capital of Australia. The capital is Canberra.

Sports: A story in some editions April 4 about the NCAA women's basketball semifinals referred incorrectly to the historical significance of Stanford's last four Final Four appearances. The team is the third to make four consecutive trips to the Final Four without winning a title.

News: A story March 31 on Japan's nuclear crisis misstated the level of radioactive iodine in seawater offshore from the Fukushima Dai-ichi Nuclear Power Station. It was 3,335 times the legal limit.

News: In a story March 30 about the fight against militants in the Philippines, the title of Brig. Gen. Edward Reeder was incorrect. He is head of Army Special Forces Command.

News: A story March 29 about proposed legislation that would reallocate some radio frequencies for use by first responders responsible for public safety incorrectly said those frequencies were the ones currently used by ham radio operators.

News: Detroit's population has dropped below that of Jacksonville, Fla. An article March 23 on Michigan Census data incorrectly identified the city that now is larger than Detroit.

Sports: A Snapshot March 29 showing baseball players with the most consecutive 200-hit seasons omitted two players who had five such years. Al Simmons and Charlie Gehringer joined Chuck Klein in that category.

Sports: A photo caption March 24 with a story about Butler forward Matt Howard misidentified the site of the team's NCAA tournament game on March 19 against Pittsburgh. It was Washington.

Life: Woody Allen and Mia Farrow were longtime partners but were not married. A story March 22 on celebrity scandals misstated the relationship.

Opinion: A column March 22 by DeWayne Wickham on the documentary Fab Five should have said the film was made by ESPN.

Opinion: A March 16 editorial on energy choices should have said that the Environmental Protection Agency is studying whether the "fracking" process to produce natural gas has contaminated drinking water and groundwater, not that studies have shown contamination.

Money: A story March 16 should have said Demand Media pays $15 to $100 for freelance articles.

News: In some editions March 17, the cover story graphic should have noted that American donations for the March 11 Japan earthquake totaled $49 million. The two-week total after 9/11was $607 million. Sandra Miniutti's name was misspelled in the story in some editions.

News: A story in some editions March 17 on the NPR expose misstated the age of James O'Keefe, political activist and video maker. He is 26.

News: A story March 17 on the United Arab Emirates misattributed a quote. It was Arab Medical Union Vice President Ali Numairi whose quote began, "Nationals enjoy many advantages and profit from Social Security and housing benefits."

News: A story March 17 on foreigners leaving Japan should have said California native Cameron Moller was leaving the country with his family. Moller plans to return to Japan next week; his family will return in two weeks.

News: A Newsline item in some editions March 16 should have said Clemson and UNC-Asheville won first-round games to advance in teh NCAA tournament.

News: A story Feb. 23 about Somali pirates misidentified the USS Bulkeley, one of the U.S. Navy ships that shadowed the hijacked yacht Quest.

Sports: A story and list March 16 about schools that have both men's and women's teams in the NCAA basketball tournament omitted one school. Princeton's teams are in together for the first time, bringing the total to 28 schools.

Sports: An item March 14 in Dick Vitale's college basketball column included an incorrect nickname for Kansas State. It is the Wildcats.

Sports: A headline March 15 on a baseball notes column included an incorrect surname for an Oakland Athletics pitcher. He is Andrew Bailey.

Life: Northwestern University's Feinberg School of Medicine is in Chicago. The location was incorrect in a story March 15 about Alzheimer's caregivers.

Sports: A photo caption March 15 with a story about Florida Marlins coach Perry Hill misstated the position of rookie Matt Dominguez. He is a third baseman.

Sports: A story March 15 about a new video game in which tennis player Andre Agassi appears mistakenly described it as his first. Agassi has appeared in multiple games.

News: In some editions of the March 14 Nationline, the owner of the Waterfront restaurant that drifted into the Ohio River in Covington, Ky., was misidentified. He is Jeff Ruby.

Life: A story March 14 about Daniel Radcliffe incorrectly attributed a quote about the actor. David Heyman, who produced the Harry Potter films, described him as "an incredibly curious person. He always pushes himself, never inhabits the safe zone. He's not one to sit quietly and do nothing."

Life: In a story March 4 on 10 great places to go dog sledding, the name of the Outward Bound dog-sledding course in Ely, Minn., was incorrect. The contact information was also incorrect; it is 866-846-7745 or outwardbound.org.

Life: A comment attributed to librarian Kelly Clever in a March 8 story about e-books and libraries should have been attributed to librarian Barbara Fister of Gustavus Adolphus College in a blog item she wrote for Library Journal.

News: A March 7 cover story about school testing should have said that Stanton Elementary School, a District of Columbia public school, is being managed in partnership with Scholar Academies, a Philadelphia-based operator of charter schools. Stanton itself is not a charter school.

Money: A preview of the iPad 2 March 3 should have said the Personal Hotspot feature of iOS 4.3 is an iPhone4-only feature.

Money: A March 1 list of the nation's best airport restaurants should have given the location of Ike's Food and Cocktails as inside the Mall of Minneapolis-St. Paul airport near Concourse E.

News: A March 7 Washington briefs item misidentified the network that Sen. Dick Durbin, D-Ill., appeared on. It was Fox News Sunday.

Sports: A story March 1 about North Carolina defensive end Robert Quinn's performance at the NFL scouting combine referred imprecisely to the reason behind his suspension last season. The NCAA found that Quinn had received improper benefits from an agent, though not his current one.

Sports: A story March 2 about high-tech devices that help troops pinpoint snipers gave the wrong rank for David Petraeus. The story should have said that Petraeus was a major general when he first requested the devices eight years ago when Iraq casualties were mounting.

Opinion: An editorial March 2 on sexual assault in the military should have said that former Marine corporal Sarah Albertson was threatened with a charge of "inappropriate barracks conduct," not charged with that offense, after reporting that she'd been raped. Albertson says the attack occurred after she was carried to the assailant's room at the barracks, not in her bed as the editorial stated.

News: A caption with a story Feb. 28 on the most recent government shutdown had an incorrect date. The photo was from Jan. 3, 1996.

Sports: A story Feb. 25 analyzing the impact of deadline trades on the NBA's Eastern Conference included an incorrect nickname for the team in Charlotte. It is the Bobcats.

Money: ProShares UltraShort DJ-UBS Crude Oil (ticker: SCO) was misidentified in a Feb. 25 Investing column.

News: A photo caption in a story Feb. 24 on Wisconsin's budget crisis misidentified a person discussing the issue. Retiree Dick McMaster was pictured.

Sports: A promotional item Feb. 24 misidentified the network of college basketball analyst Greg Anthony. It is CBS Sports.

News: A story Feb. 18 on efforts to fight fraud in the Recovery Act misstated the amount of the stimulus. It is $814 billion.

Sports: A story and headline Feb. 21 about the performance of Richard Childress Racing in the Daytona 500 misstated the number of entries the team had in the race. There were four, including two cars that were forced out early.

Sports: A story Feb. 18 about the Arizona Diamondbacks misstated the number of times coach Matt Williams made the All-Star team. He was named five times.

News: A photo caption with a story Feb. 21 on the resettlement of Burmese refugees misidentified the location of Butterworth Enterprises. It's in Gas City, Ind.

Life: Reckon, a red merle Australian shepherd, is an American Kennel Club championship winner. A Feb. 14 story about the Westminster Kennel Club Dog Show was incorrect.

Life: Earl Spencer, who announced he will wed this summer, is the late Princess Diana's younger brother. An item in a Feb. 16 Life section mistakenly identified him.

Life: A story Feb. 15 about CBS' Survivor misstated the number of contestants. There are 18 total, including the returning Rob Mariano and Russell Hantz.

News: Several states lack a national park, but Delaware is the only one without a National Park Service park, monument or other site. The Delaware item in Across the USA on Feb. 14 was incorrect.

News: A Feb. 15 story on Christians in Egypt should have said the date of an attack on a church in Alexandria was Jan. 1.

News: A story Feb. 15 on the State Department's use of Twitter to encourage opposition groups in Iran mischaracterized comments by department spokesman Mike Hammer. He did not address whether the government of President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad should be overthrown.

Life: A report Feb. 14 about the Grammy Awards identified Train as a first-time winner, but members of the band won best rock song for Drops of Jupiter. The track also won an award for arranger Paul Buckmaster. A Feb. 11 Grammy ballot incorrectly noted that best new artist has never been won by a teen; the award has never been won by a teen-oriented act.

Sports: A story Feb. 14 about the Boston Celtics' victory against the Miami Heat misstated in some editions the length of teh Heat's winning streak entering the game. It was eight games.

News: The name of the travel website Gogobot was misspelled in an item on Page 1A on Feb. 14.

Sports: A story Feb. 9 about changes to NASCAR competition this year misstated the amount of fresh asphalt used to resurface the track at Daytona International Speedway. It was 50,000 tons.

News: A Nationline item Feb. 9 misstated some details in the case against Jessica Blackham, who was accused of leaving an infant she had just delivered in a South Carolina public restroom. Blackham is 24, faces one count of felony child abuse and one count of unlawful neglect toward a child. She could face up to 30 years in prison. The Associated Press provided the erroneous information.

News: A story Feb. 8 on AmeriCorps misstated the scope of the agency's national service program. It spends $698 million annually to support 88,530 members.

Sports: A story Feb. 3 about twin brothers who signed to play football at Notre Dame referred incorrectly to their high school. George and Josh Atkinson attend Granada High School in Livermore, Calif.

News: In some editions Feb. 4, the chart on key players in Egypt misidentified the TV network that got an interview with President Hosni Mubarak. It was ABC.

Life: A story Feb. 3 gave the wrong Motion Picture Association of America rating for The Social Network. The film is rated PG-13.

Life: A photo caption in some editions Feb. 3 on winter moods misstated the location of Oskaloosa. It's in Iowa.

Opinion: A photo caption accompanying a letters package Feb. 3 gave the incorrect reason a plane made an emergency landing in Colorado Springs on Dec. 30. It was diverted because of an engine problem.

Sports: A story Feb. 1 about the cost of attending the Super Bowl included an incorrect range for the price of a four-day, three-night trip from Pittsburgh or Milwaukee to Dallas. SeatGeek said the per-person costs are $5,106 at the low end and $16,328 at the high end.

Sports: A story Feb. 1 about Pittsburgh Steelers quarterback Ben Roethlisberger referred incorrectly to comments made by NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell about his investigation that led to Roethlisberger's four-game suspension. Goodell said that before reaching a decision, he had talked to an estimated two dozen NFL players, none of whom had come to the quarterback's defense. The original reference cited a report by Sports Illustrated's Peter King that indicated the players were all Steelers. Late night on Jan. 31, King issued a clarification in which he said Goodell was not referring to the Steelers specifically.

Sports: A story Feb. 1 about Toronto Raptors forward DeMar DeRozan misstated one of his scoring statistics in some editions. He has had 14 games of 20 or more points, including 11 since Dec. 1.

Life: A cover story Jan. 27 on Army Capt. Patrick Horan, who is recovering from a gunshot wound to the head, incorrectly identified David Patterson, the medical director of Casa Colina Centers for Rehabilitation in Pomona, Calif. He is a physiatrist.

Sports: A listing Jan. 31 of results from the U.S. Figure Skating Championships included incorrect free-skate scores for two pairs. Caydee Denney and Jeremy Barrett were third with 117.70 points, and Mary Beth Marley and Rockne Brubaker were fourth with 105.45.

Money: The cover story Jan. 31 should have said that light bulb maker ThinkLite has established credit lines with nine custom manufacturers as it continues to grow its business.

Opinion: An item on Jan. 31's Forum page had the incorrect birthday for President Reagan. It is Feb. 6, 1911.

Sports: The world golf rankings Jan. 27 included incorrect nationalities for five players. They are: No 9. Paul Casey, England; 10. Luke Donald, England; 11. Ernie Els, South Africa; 12. Ian Poulter, England; and 14. Retief Goosen, South Africa.

Sports: A story Jan. 28 about Green Bay Packers linebacker A.J. Hawk misstated the relationship of his wife, the former Laura Quinn, to NFL quarterback Brady Quinn. She is his sister.

Sports: A cover story Jan. 28 about Dallas Cowboys owner Jerry Jones referred incorrectly to the season in which former coach Barry Switzer won the Super Bowl. It was his second season, 1995-1996. The story also misstated the duration of Switzer's tenure with the team. He coached four seasons.

News: A 1A cover story Jan. 28 about Baby Boomers engaging in encore careers cited an incorrect figure for the Farm to Family program in California. The program distributed more than 100 million pounds of fresh produce in 2010. The story also mispelled Worcester, Mass.

News: The mayor of Philadelphia is Michael Nutter. His name was misspelled in a storm story Jan. 27.

News: A story Jan. 26 misstated the nature of the flight that astronaut Alan Shepard took as the first American in space in 1961. His flight was suborbital. Also, a former student of Christa McAuliffe's, Matthew Mead, was incorrectly identified in a headline.

Money: A story Jan. 25 about discount grocers had an incorrect name for the company that publishes the Times & Trends report. It is the SymphonyIRI Group. Also, the number of months over which Times & Trends reported consumers struggling to afford groceries had increased by 11 percentage points was incorrect. It was six months.

News: A news brief Jan. 25 misstated when the Roe v. Wade decision was issued. It was Jan. 22, 1973.

Sports: A headline with a story Jan. 24 about the sixth-seeded Green Bay Packers' victory in the NFC championship game misstated in some editions the record of No. 6 seeds in the NFL playoffs. Although the AFC's Pittsburgh Steelers won the Super Bowl after the 2005 season as a sixth seed, no NFC team has matched that.

Money: A story Jan. 20 about Quora should have said that it has 16 employees.

News: A story Jan. 20 about military suicides misstated the number of confirmed or suspected suicides among Marines in 2010. The number is 37.

Sports: A chart Jan. 17 showing college football coaching changes omitted one piece of information about Colorado's new coach, Jon Embree. He is a minority.

Sports: A tale-of-the-tape look Jan. 19 at the New York Jets-Pittsburgh Steelers AFC title game and the teams' home regions included imprecise information in two photo captions. Although inventor Thomas Edison spent most of his professional life in New Jersey, he was born in Milan, Ohio; and although Jonas Salk was born in New York, he developed the polio vaccine at the University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine.

Sports: The Super 25 boys basketball rankings Jan. 18 included an incorrect record for No. 17 Lincoln (Brooklyn, N.Y.). It was 15-1 following a 63-45 loss to Christian Brothers Academy (Lincroft, N.J.).

Sports: A story Jan. 18 about horse racing's Eclipse Awards misidentified in some editions the period when Cigar was named horse of the year. He won consecutive crowns in 1995 and 1996.

Sports: The 2011 motor sports schedule Jan. 14 misidentified the site of the June 25 Nationwide Series race. It is Elkhart Lake, Wis. Also, the time of the April 30 Sprint Cup race at Richmond International Raceway was incorrect. The race starts at 7:30 p.m.

Sports: The for-the-record section Jan. 12 and Jan. 13 referred incorrectly to the site of the Heineken Open tennis event. It is Auckland, New Zealand.

News: The image that accompanied a Jan. 11 story about the discovery of the planet Kepler-10b was an artist's rendering.

Life: Erin Brockovich writer Susannah Grant received a Nicholl Fellowship in screenwriting from the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences in 1992. A story Jan. 11 incorrectly described the prize.

News: A story Jan. 11 about Judy Clarke, attorney for Tucson shooting suspect Jared Loughner, misstated the college where she and colleague David Bruck teach law. It is Washington and Lee University in Virginia.

Sports: A cover story Jan. 7 about the NFL playoffs misidentified in some editions the team for which defensive end Will Smith plays. It is the New Orleans Saints.

Money: A story Jan. 5 should have said that Motorola shareholders of record as of Dec. 21 on Jan. 4 received one share of Motorola Mobility for every eight shares of Motorola held. Then, in a reverse stock split, those same shareholders received one share of Motorola for every seven shares of Motorola held. Finally, Motorolla was then renamed Motorola Solutions.

Sports: A Snapshot on Jan. 3 listing the highest-scoring Orange Bowls referred incorrectly to the number of times the game has gone into overtime. In addition to Michigan's 35-34 victory against Alabama in 2000, Penn State defeated Florida State 26-23 in triple overtime in 2006.

Sports: An item in the Heat Wave column Jan. 4 referred incorrectly in some editions to the game situation when the Miami Heat's Big 3 of LeBron James, Dwyane Wade and Chris Bosh went to the bench the night of Jan 3. Those players alone had outscored the Charlotte Bobcats 80-76 as the Heat built a 94-76 lead on the way to a 96-82 victory.

Money: A chart on 1B Jan. 3 misstated the S&P 500's decline in 2008. It fell 38.5%.

News: In the Dec. 30 issue, passages on notable deaths misstated when Philadelphia Phillies broadcaster Harry Kalas died. It was April 13, 2009.

Sports: The final Super 25 high school football rankings Dec. 21 included an incorrect record for No. 18 Cocoa (Fla.). It was 14-0.

Sports: A headline Dec. 30 with a news brief about the departure of swim coach Sean Hutchinson referred incorrectly to the program he had left. It was a swim club that had no affiliation with the University of Southern California.

Sports: The Sports on TV column Dec. 29 misstated the bowl game that Connecticut would be playing in on New Year's Day. it was the Fiesta Bowl. Also, the column referred incorrectly to the postseason status of Texas. Because of its 5-7 record, Texas was not eligible for a bowl.

Opinion: Dec. 30's "resolutions we'd like to see" should have described Juan Williams as a former analyst for NPR, not PBS.

News: A caption Dec. 28 accompanying a photo of Liao Zhongtao, a member of a Chinese leper colony, misstated her gender.

News: The New Hampshire item in Dec. 29's Across the USA misidentified the Kearsarge Regional School District.

News: A Nationline item Dec. 28 on a New Mexico city's proposed ban on synthetic marijuana incorrectly linked it to salvia divinorum, which is a hallucinogenic herb.

News: A caption accompanying a story Dec. 27 about body scanners at airports misidentified the type of machine shown at San Francisco International Airport. It is a body scanner that does not emit X-rays.

Money: A story on used SUV prices in Dec. 28 editions was previously published in May 2008 and contained outdated material — including interviews. Prices for used SUVs are actually down 1.5% since January 2010. Chrysler is no longer offering a promotion guaranteeing gas prices.

Sports: Dec. 28's TV ratings chart should have noted that Sunday's Redskins-Jaguars NFL game was down 26% from comparable coverage of last year's Ravens-Steelers game.

News: A 1A map Dec. 22 about the Census 2010 population growth should have identified Georgia, which will gain one seat in the House of Representatives.

News: An "Across the USA" item Dec. 17 about the struggle of Springfield's Gillioz Theatre to avoid foreclosure should have been listed under news from Missouri.

Life: To take the flirting survey created by Jeffrey Hall at the University of Kansas, go to connect.ku.edu/tests/flirt. the URL was incorrect in a Dec. 20 story about his flirting research.

Sports: A story Dec. 20 about the Connecticut women's basketball team referred incorrectly in some editions to the class of Huskies guard Tiffany Hayes. She is a junior

Sports: The college football roundup Dec. 20 included a misspelled given name for a Wisconsin-Whitewater running back whose touchdown sealed the team's Division III title win. He is Levell Coppage.

Sports: A news brief Dec. 20 about the St. John's women's basketball victory against UC-Santa Barbara included an incorrect ranking for the winner in some editions. St. John's is No. 18 in the latest USA TODAY/ESPN Coaches Poll.

Sports: The NFL cheat sheet Dec. 20 included an incorrect score for the Kansas City Chiefs-St. Louis Rams game in some editions. The Chiefs won 27-13.

Sports: The Sports on TV column Dec. 20 referred imprecisely in some editions to Washington Redskins quarterback Donovan McNabb's league awards. He is a six-time Pro Bowler.

News: A Dec. 14 story on natural gas drilling mischaracterized a recent incident in Dimock, Pa., in which methane gas seeped into the underground water supply. The incident occurred during a preliminary stage of drilling, before a controversial process known as hydraulic fracturing was used to extract natural gas, according to John Hanger, secretary of the Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection.

Sports: A chart Dec. 15 showing the 2010-11 college football bowl schedule included an incorrect record for Troy, which faces Ohio (8-4) in the Dec.18 New Orleans Bowl. It is 7-5.

News: A story in Dec. 14 editions about Afghan women receiving scholarships to U.S. colleges had an incorrect location for Dickinson College. It is in Carlisle, Pa. The story also misidentified the residence of one of the program's founders. Joseph Highland is from Princeton, N.J.

News: A story in some editions Dec. 10 misstated the number of people killed by a gunman at Virginia Tech on April 16, 2007. It was 32.

Life: A Dec. 13 story on the movie Fast Five, starring Vin Diesel and Dwayne Johnson, gave the incorrect release date. The next installment in the Furious franchise arrives April 29.

Money: A story Dec. 13 on efforts to crack down on firms that misclassify workers as independent contractors misstated the amount of back wages the Labor Department forced employers to pay. The amount was $6.5 million in fiscal 2010, up from $2.6 million a year earlier.

Life: An item Dec. 9 gave the incorrect publication date for Richelle Mead's Bloodines. The novel will be published in August.

Sports: A story Dec. 8 about unusual provisions in some college football coaches' contracts mistakenly attributed some incentives to the contract of Army coach Rich Ellerson in some editions. It is Bowling Green coach Dave Clawson whose contract calls for a $10,000 payment each year if he plays a regular-season game against a team from a BCS conference and Bowling Green receives at least $400,000 for the game.

Sports: A cover story Dec. 7 about licensing high school athletics gear referred incorrectly to a clothing article for toddlers by using a trademarked name. The item should have been described as a one-piece outfit.

News: a story Dec. 8 about Democrats' anger toward President Obama misidentified the state where the Progressive Change Campaign Committee is running television ads opposed to extending tax cuts for top earners. It is Iowa.

News: In some Dec. 2 editions, a photo caption accompanying a cover story on LeBron James misidentified the man selling T-shirts. His name is Derrick Tatum.

Money: A story Dec. 1 should have said that some 3-D games are playable on Xbox 360 and that the release date for Killzone 3 is Feb. 22.

News: A story Nov. 28 on charity donations going digital misspelled the last names of Angelo Biasi, vice president of marketing for app software company DIDMO, and Patrick Mork, chief marketing officer of app store GetJar.

Sports: An NHL roundup brief in some editions Dec. 1 misstated Marian Hossa's current team. He's with the Chicago Blackhawks.

Money:Securities attorney Dan Hurson of Daniel J. Hurson Law Offices was misquoted in a story Nov. 26 about Wall Street investigations of insider trading. He was referring to the U.S. Attorney for New York in the comment, "They play hardball."

Sports: A list Nov. 26 of Super 25 high school football rematches omitted No. 12 Hoover, Ala., which defeated Mountain Brook (Birmingham) 24-3 on Oct. 22 in the regular season and 44-20 in the 6A playoffs Nov. 26.

News: The student body president at Drake University in Des Moines is Samantha Haas. A story Nov. 26 about the public's faith in the American Dream incorrectly identified her.

Sports: A news brief Nov. 24 about Miami (Ohio) defeating Temple in football referred incorrectly in some editions to the impact of the win on the Mid-American Conference race. Miami clinched a share of the Eastern Division title, but Ohio will advance to the MAC championship game with a victory today.

Sports: A column Nov. 19 about former NFL official Mike Pereira's role at Fox Sports misstated the date of the AT&T Cotton Bowl. It is Jan. 7.

Sports: A news brief in some editions Nov. 19 about Michigan State basketball coach Tom Izzo's new contract misstated the year in which he led the team to an NCAA title. It was 1999. Also, Kansas won in 2008.

Sports: A story Nov. 19 previewing the final race in the NASCAR Chase misstated the year in which the Richard Childress Racing team won a title. It was 1994.

Life: Explorer and hunter Carl Akeley was standing next to a leopard he killed with his bare hands in a photo in the Nov. 18 paper. Also, the photo should have been credited to the Field Museum.

News: A story Nov. 19 on a homeless man who found a backpack containing $3,300 misidentified the supervisor who discovered the backpack's owner. The supervisor was Sam Sumner.

Sports: A story Nov. 18 about an expanded baseball playoff system misidentified the team for which Walt Jocketty serves as general manager. It is the Cincinnati Reds.

News: A story Nov. 17 about college students' drinking behavior incorrectly identified the affiliation of ophthalmology professor David Granet. He is with the University of California-San Diego.

Sports: A story Nov. 11 previewing weekend motor sports action included an incorrect given name for a Pro Stock Motorcycle rider in some editions. He is Andrew Hines. Also, a photo accompanying the story was incorrectly described in the caption. The Abu Dhabi Top Fuel car that was shown is driver Rod Fuller's.

News and Money: Nov 10 editions had incorrect photos on 1A and 2B of the Ford F-150 and Dodge Ram 1500 as tested for the V-6 Work Truck Shoot-out.

News: An article Nov. 9 about immigration policies in New Mexico mischaracterized Univision. It is a Spanish-language broadcast network.

Opinion: A letter published Nov. 10 incorrectly calculated the amount of money that would be collected if people contributed $400 million a year for 10 years to pay down the national debt. The donations would total $4 billion.

Sports: A story Nov. 5 about the death of former baseball manager Sparky Anderson misstated the year of the Detroit Tigers' most recent title before 1984. They won the World Series in 1968.

Life: Peter Gammons, guest editor of The Best American Sports Writing 2010, is an analyst with MLB Network, MLB.com and NESN. His affiliation was incorrect in a book review Nov. 4.

Sports: A news brief Nov. 3 about the NBA's annual roster survey included incorrect information that was in the report. the league's heaviest player is Boston Celtics center Shaquille O'Neal at 325, not Charlotte Bobcats center DeSagana Diop, who weighs 280.

News: A 1A headline in an early edition Nov. 3 misidentified Republican Marco Rubio. He won the race for U.S. Senate in Florida.

Life: In a story Nov. 3 about "Cake Boss" Buddy Valastro, the visit to his bakery of a 7-year-old girl with cancer was sponsored by hMAG (h-mag.com) of Hoboken, N.J. The sponsor was misidentified in the story.

Sports: A Sportsline list of NFL scores Nov. 1 included an incorrect result in some editions for the Minnesota-New England game. New England won 28-18.

News: Lynda Lovejoy, a candidate for president of the Navajo Nation, is 61. Her age was incorrect in a story Oct. 29.

Money: Filmmaker Eric Kurland works in a 3-D studio that is not located at his home. The location was wrong in a story Oct. 27 about 3-D video.

Sports: A cover story Oct 28 about the Michigan State football team referred incorrectly to its votes in the preseason polls. The team received 10 points in the USA TODAY Coaches Poll and none in the Associated Press media poll.

News: Tommy Ray is a special agent with the Florida Department of Law Enforcement. His agency was misidentified in a story Oct. 26 on the use of coasters and cards to collect crime tips.

Life: The photograph accompanying the Oct. 26 review of the play Driving Miss Daisy was incorrectly credited. The photograph was taken by Annabel Clark.

Sports: An Oct. 26 story about New Orleans Hornets coach Monty Williams referred incorrectly to the team's recent playoff history. The Hornets failed to make the playoffs last season but did reach the postseason the previous season.

News: A story about the Ohio governor's race in some Oct. 25 editions misidentified the city where Garland Gates is a Democratic councilman. It is Shelby, Ohio.

News: The security features of a new U.S. naturalization certificate unveiled on Oct. 25 do not include a holographic image or laser-engraved fingerprint as reported in a cutline and the Newsline in some Oct. 25 editions.

News: In some Oct. 11 editions, Florida Gov. Charlie Crist's name was misspelled in a story about governors using YouTube.

Sports: A photograph Oct. 21 showing a play at first base in a New York Yankees-Texas Rangers playoff game was presented as if it was from the Oct. 20 night matchup. The action was from the Oct. 19 game.

Sports: A cover story Oct. 21 about NFL labor talks included in some editions a misspelled given name for a sports marketing consultant. He is Marc Ganis.

News: Voters nationwide on Nov. 2 will consider the smallest number of bond issues in an even-year election since 1996, according to The Bond Buyer, which tracks government finance. A story Oct. 18 misstated the year.

Sports: A chart Oct. 15 showing weekend college football matchups included an incorrect reference to the playing surface at TCU's Amon G. Carter Stadium. It is grass.

Life: In a photo caption accompanying a story Oct. 14 about the Peace Corps, President Kennedy is shown May 18, 1963, addressing the first group of volunteers leaving for Indonesia.

News: A 1A cover story Oct. 15 about citizen videos increasing scrutiny of police misspelled Harford County, Md.

Sports: A headline Oct. 14 on the NBA notes incorrectly referred to the experience of Nick Young. He is in his fourth season.

Sports: A Sportsline item Oct. 14 incorrectly implied that David Pearson, Cale Yarborough and Darrell Waltrip were the only Hall of Fame candidates with three NASCAR Cup championships. New Hall inductee Lee Petty also had three championships.

Money: In a photo caption accompanying a story Oct. 13 about the Whrrl mobile app, Pelago CEO Jeff Holden is shown with an iPhone, not an Android phone.

Sports: The motor sports standings Oct. 7 referred incorrectly to the number of wins Formula One driver Mark Webber has this season. He has four.

Money: Ford Motor sells a four-door hatchback, also called a five-door, version of the Fiesta small car. A story Oct. 8 was incorrect.

News: Patrick Gasper's name was misspelled in an Oct. 7 story about high school mascot name changes.

News: Harvard student Sylvia Rodriguez and her family entered the USA legally but stayed after their visas expired. A story Oct. 6 described their entry incorrectly.

Life: The last name of the owners of T-Ray's Burger Station on Amelia Island in Florida was misspelled in a story Oct. 1. The owners are Big Ray and T-Ray Mullis.

News: A story Sept. 30 about Israeli settlements gave an incorrect location for where the Bible says God first spoke to Abraham. It was in Mesopotamia.

News: An Across the USA item Oct. 6 about a coal-mining lawsuit in Flagstaff should have been listed under Arizona.

Money: Nissan Versa and Juke small cars aren't based on the same platform, or chassis. A story Oct. 6 was incorrect.

News: A story Sept. 30 about Israeli settlements gave the incorrect name for the alliance that defeated the Ottoman Empire. It was the Allied Powers.

Money: Hardship withdrawals from 401(k) plans to pay for college tuition are subject to income taxes and the 10% early withdrawal penalty, unless the plan participant is 59 1/2 or older. An Oct. 5 Your Money column contained incorrect information about the withdrawal penalty.

Sports: The list Oct. 4 of weekend college football scores included an incorrect score for the Temple-Army game. Temple won 42-35.

Sports: A story Oct. 4 about baseball's playoff picture referred incorrectly to the scenario in which both the San Francisco Giants and San Diego Padres both would have qualified for the playoffs on Oct. 3. That would have occurred only if the Padres had won and the Atlanta Braves had lost.

News: A cover story Sept. 27 about former president John Kennedy included an incorrect age for former White House adviser Ted Sorensen. He is 82.

News: A Nationline brief Sept. 30 should have said a record number of children this year have died in hot cars from hyperthermia.

Sports: A photo Sept. 29 of former Duke track coach Al Buehler carried an incorrect credit. It was provided by The News & Observer of Raleigh.

Sports: A headline Sept. 29 with a news brief about recommendations that sports federations adopt certain conduct standards misidentified the organization that offered the proposal. It was the U.S. Olympic Committee.

Sports: A Snapshot Sept. 29 about postseason appearances by baseball managers included in some editions an incorrect spelling of the surname of one of the managers. He is Casey Stengel.

Sports: The NFL Week 3 top performances list Sept. 27 omitted two rushers. The Pittsburgh Steelers' Rashard Mendenhall ran for 143 yards vs. the Tampa Bay Buccaneers and the Tennessee Titans' Chris Johnson's had 125 yards vs. the New York Giants.

Sports: The Associated Press college football poll in the Sept. 27 paper had two teams in incorrect positions because an out-of-date ballot was used, according to the AP. Michigan is No. 19, and South Carolina No. 20.

Sports: A story Sept. 27 about U.S. Olympic Committee chairman Larry Probst misreported his age and position at Electronic Arts. Probst, 60, is the chairman of Electronic Arts.

News: A photo caption Sept. 27 of U.S. troops patrolling in Afghanistan gave an incorrect rank for Marine Lance Cpl. Damien Griego.

Life: The fall music preview Sept. 17 gave the wrong age for Lil Wayne. The rapper will be 28 on Sept. 27, the release date of his new album, I'm Not a Human Being.

Sports: A story Sept. 21 about maple baseball bats referred incorrectly to the name of a bat manufacturer in some editions. It is Hillerich & Bradsby.

Sports: The Today's line column Sept. 16included incorrect odds for the Shane Mosley-Sergio Mora junior middleweight fight. Mosley is favored 2:1.

Sports: The NFL team-by-team notes Sept. 16 omitted an entry for the Pittsburgh Steelers in some editions. It can be accessed at nfl.usatoday.com.

Sports: A story Sept. 15 about NCAA enforcement erroneously included Clemson among schools under investigation. The school and Atlantic Coast Conference say there is no ongoing probe.

Money: A cover story Sept. 10 should have said that Anthony and April Soper of Lake Stevens, Wash., received a modified mortgage from Bank of America that reduced their total mortgage

News: In a 1A story Sept. 14 about school breakfast programs, the name of Colorado Gov. Bill Ritter was incorrect.

Sports: A story Sept. 14 about an incident involving TV Azteca reporter Ines Sainz and the New York Jets incorrectly linked her to two situations that involved other people: proposing to New England Patriots quarterback Tom Brady during Super Bowl Week 2008 and representing Spain in the 1997 Miss Universe pageant.

Sports: The NFL Preview bonus section Sept. 10 included conflicting ages for Chicago Bears quarterback Jay Cutler. He is 27.

News: An article published Sept. 3 on American Muslims who lost relatives in the 9/11 terrorist attacks incorrectly described the religion of Michael Baksh, who was killed at the World Trade Center. He was a member of the Seventh-day Adventist Church.

News: A graphic Sept. 7 about the makeup of the U.S. House of Representatives should have made clear that of the two vacancies, one seat was held previously by a Republican and the other by a Democrat.

News: A front page story on health care costs Sept. 2 misspelled the last name of Robert Book, a health care economist at the Heritage Foundation.

News: A caption with a photo Sept. 2 of a woman mourning over a migrant killed in Mexico misstated the location of Tegucigalpa. It is in Honduras.

News: Richard Land is president of the Southern Baptist Ethics & Religious Liberty Commission. The name of the commission was incomplete in a story Aug. 31 on a fire at a Tennessee mosque project.

News: Army Pfc. Bryn T. Raver died after a rocket-propelled-grenade attack Aug. 28 in Afghanistan's Nangarhar province. His name was misspelled in a casualty list in some editions Sept. 1.

News: A 1A story Aug. 27 on the ethnic makeup of today's kindergartners misstated the year that most will graduate from high school. It is 2023.

Life: A story Aug. 26 on archaeological exploration of Maya ruins in Mexico misspelled the name of Millsaps College of Jackson, Miss.

News: Richard Land is president of the Southern Baptist Ethics & Religious Liberty Commission. The name of the commission was incomplete in a story Aug. 31.

Money:A snapshot published Aug. 24 misstated the results of a survey by financial services firm Edward Jones. It found 65% of those surveyed would not invest in oil stocks if they had the money, 33% said they would and 2% said they didn't know.

Money: A story Aug. 25 about iTunes account hijacking misstated the name of Randy Abrams, education director for anti-virus firm ESET.

Sports: A preview Aug. 25 of the NCAA college football Championship Subdivision referred incorrectly to Richmond coach Latrell Scott's age, relative to that of his fellow coaches. Scott, 35, is the third youngest coach in Division I.

Opinion: An Aug. 24 editorial on Avastin should have said the drug was approved for treating kidney and brain cancer in 2009, not several years ago.

Opinion: A graphic with an Aug. 20 flooding editorial overstated the percentage of Pakistanis who view the USA favorably. When people who replied "don't know" or refused to answer are subtracted, the correct favorability ratings were 10% in 2002, 27% in 2006 and 17% in 2010.

Life: Tony DiTerlizzi's forthcoming book is called The Search for WondLa. The title was incorrect in a story Aug. 19.

News: A story Aug. 18 about a survey by auto club AAA of drivers distracted by dogs as passengers should have noted that the cost of the survey by an independent research firm was split by the auto club and Kurgo, a company that sells harnesses and other products to protect dogs, drivers and passengers on the road.

News: A story Aug. 16 about a demonstration in support of Arizona's new immigration law misstated the location of Hereford, Ariz., in some editions. It is about 70 miles east of Nogales. The information was provided by the Associated Press.

News: An Aug. 4 story about Elena Kagan's Supreme Court nomination incorrectly described the findings of a USA TODAY/Gallup poll. Since 46% supported Kagan, the story should have said that many poll respondents were behind her.

Sports: The Game On! column Aug. 18 included an incorrect given name in some editions for a boxer who was mentioned in an item about promoter Don King's birthday. He is Floyd Mayweather Jr.

Sports: A story Aug. 16 about former Olympic figure skating bronze medalist Debi Thomas, who is now an orthopedic surgeon, was accompanied by an incorrect photo.

Sports: An Aug. 16 story about Washington Redskins running backs referred incorrectly to the status of Clinton Portis and Larry Johnson in the team's preseason opener against the Buffalo Bills. Portis carried the ball six times for 22 yards; Johnson did not play.

Sports: An Aug. 16 story about Martin Kaymer's PGA Championship victory, and an accompanying photo caption, included a comment from him that referred incorrectly to past PGA champions. Although Arnold Palmer won seven majors, the PGA was the one title that eluded him.

News: Antwoine Marshall survived an attack by an alleged serial killer July 27 in Flint, Mich. An Aug. 13 story gave an incorrect date.

News: An Aug. 13 story on high airfares should have said fares in July were 10% higher on domestic routes and 17% higher on international routes than last July. Fares for the second quarter were up 20% from a year earlier.

Life: Because Angelina, Andrew Morton's unauthorized biography of Angelina Jolie, went on sale July 31, the first two days of sales were not reflected in USA TODAY's Best-Selling Books list the week of Aug. 9. The list counts sales Mondays through Sundays. That information was incorrect in the Aug. 12 Book Buzz column.

Sports: An Aug. 12 news brief about the Bellator Fighting Championships referred incorrectly in some editions to heavyweight fighter Josh Barnett. He is a former UFC champion.

Sports: An Aug. 12 Page One cover story about allegations of sexual misconduct by swimming coaches omitted details about Mike Saltzstein's relationship with USA Swimming. Saltzstein, who was vice president with the organization from 2000 to 2006, filed a complaint with the U.S. Olympic Committee claiming USA Swimming unfairly denied him work after he criticized it for its handling of sexual abuse cases. An arbitrator ruled in Saltzstein's favor last month.

News: A Page One story Aug. 9 about military mentors did not provide details on how $50,000 in extra pay could be awarded. Pentagon spokesman Bryan Whitman said, "It need not be performance based but incentive pay or some similar additional benefit for recruitment, retention or relocation purposes."

Life: In a story Aug. 6 about county fairs, the photos were taken by Drake Hokanson and Carol Kratz, authors of Purebred and Homegrown: America's County Fairs.

News: The Aug. 4 cover story on a U.S. prison in Afghanistan misidentified the military branch and rank of David Frakt. He is a lieutenant colonel in the Air Force Reserve.

Opinion: In an editorial Aug. 4, USA TODAY incorrectly reported the timing of Rep. Joseph Crowley's sponsorship of an amendment to weaken consumer protections in a financial reform measure passed by the House last December. It preceded a fundraiser for him held by a lobbyist Dec. 10, rather than following it.

Sports: An Aug. 2 brief misstated the location of Fort McNair, where President Obama played basketball Sunday against an Army team. It is in Washington, D.C.

Sports: An Aug. 2 story incorrectly listed the site of The Greenbriar Classic PGA Tour tournament. It was played in White Sulphur Springs, W.Va.

Life: A story July 30 on the Pinellas County, Fla., area incorrectly located Innisbrook's premier golf community. It is on Lake Innisbrook. Also, a photo caption incorrectly placed the high-priced home; it's on Crystal Beach.

Sports: A July 29 cover story on bat safety misstated the length and weight of a popular model of bat. It should have said a 34-inch, 29-ounce bat was one example of the models, which carried a differential of five, banned before the 2001 season by the National Federation of State High School Associations.

Money: A story July 28 on General Motors' Chevrolet Volt should have said it was the automaker's first attempt at an electric car since the EV1 in the late 1990s.

News: A story July 28 incorrectly identified the month for which Panama City Beach, Fla., tourism tax collections decreased 4.6%. The figure was actually for June.

News: A July 26 story in the newspaper on congestion-relief efforts in the Twin Cities incorrectly reported the percentage of trips on mass transit. About 2% of all trips are on mass transit.

Sports: A story in July 27 editions about Nebraska football and the school's move next year to the Big Ten referred incorrectly to Bo Pelini's record as coach there. He is 19-8 in the last two years and 20-8 overall. Also, the class year for defensive tackle Jared Crick was incorrect. He is a junior.

Sports: A photo in July 27 editions was incorrectly identified as poker player W.C. Pearson. The player pictured was Amarillo Slim Preston.

Life: The name of Cage the Elephant guitarist Brad Shultz was misspelled in a photo caption July 27.

News: A story July 26 on congestion-relief efforts in the Twin Cities incorrectly reported the percentage of trips on mass transit. About 2% of all trips are on mass transit.

Sports: A story July 22 about the resumption of the Empire State Games included in some editions a misspelled surname for one of the athletes who had competed in the games. He is basketball player Chris Mullin.

Sports: In a July 20 TV ratings chart on Page 3C, Philadelphia's opponent in ESPN's Sunday night baseball game was misidentified. The Phillies played the Chicago Cubs.

Sports: A story July 21 about the baseball trade market included a misspelled surname for a San Diego Padres first baseman. He is Adrian Gonzalez.

Sports: A story July 20 about historically weak college football programs misstated when Vanderbilt last won a conference championship. It won the Southern Conference title in 1923.

Life: The name of the National Athletic Trainer's Association was incorrect in a July 19 Your Health column.

News: An item on a July 15 Across the USA page included an incorrect party affiliation for Arkansas Gov. Mike Beebe. He is a Democrat.

Money: A story July 14 on smartphone bank deposits should have said Chase has 5,100 branches.

Money: The name of the Infiniti QX56 was misspelled in the July 9 Test Drive online.

Life: Actor Jeff Conaway's name was misspelled in a photo caption July 7 accompanying a story about the release of Grease: Sing-A-Long.

Opinion: The Center for Consumer Freedom, an advocacy group, does not advertise for or against political candidates. A July 6 editorial about the Disclose Act implied otherwise.

Sports: A photo caption accompanying a story on the Tour de France in July 1's editions misstated the reason for the pro cycling team Astana not competing in the Tour of Spain. It was not invited.

LIfe: A Lifeline column June 30 misidentified the Eminem single at No. 1 on Billboard's Hot 100 chart. Love the Way You Lie sold 338,000 downloads last week, according to Nielsen Sound-Scan; Not Afraid, the rapper's previous single, sold 132,000.

Life: Author Janet Evanovich's website is evanovich.com. That information was incorrect in a story July 1.

Money: A story July 1 should have said the average credit score of mortgage borrowers whose loans Freddie Mac buys has risen from 710 in 2001 to 751 in the first quarter.

News: French lawmaker Andre Gerin's party affiliation was misidentified in a story June 28 about proposed bans on burqas. He is a Communist. Also, a photo caption accompanying the story misstated the status of a proposed ban in France. It will be considered by the National Assembly next month.

Money: the number of aircraft equipped with Aircell's Internet service in a story June 28 on Wi-Fi on airlines was incorrect. It's available on nearly 1,000 planes.

News: A story June 24 about a new nuclear arms treaty between the U.S. and Russia incorrectly stated when the Senate Foreign Relations Committee expects to hold a vote on ratification. The panel hopes to vote before the Senate breaks for its August recess.

Sports: A story June 22 about U.S. Open golf champion Graeme McDowell being a guest on a late-night talk show referred imprecisely to the circumstances that led up to his appearance. The PGA Tour had contacted NBC's Tonight Show and CBS'Late Show with David Letterman and chose the Tonight Show when it received a firm offer.

News: A story June 21 about privately sponsored travel for members of Congress was not clear about how the Aspen Institute uses funding from the Rockefeller Foundation. It is used for staff and operational costs, not for lawmaker travel.

Sports: The age of golfer Gary Player was incorrect in a June 24 story about his new book. He is 74.

News: The name of Vincent Sheheen, the Democratic gubernatorial nominee in South Carolina, was misspelled in some editions June 23.

Opinion: A letter published June 23 incorrectly stated the amount of viable farmland in Gaza; 29% of the land is arable.

News: A photo caption in some editions June 21 misstated the winning bid for a letter from Mount Rushmore sculptor Gutzon Borglum. It was $5,826.

Money: The party affiliation of Texas Senator Kay Bailey Hutchison was incorrect in a June 22 story about whether airline mergers cause fares to rise. She is a Republican.

News: Kansas state senator David Haley is one of five candidates for the Democratic Senate nomination in the Aug. 3 Kansas primary. His name should have been included on a list that ran in USA TODAY on June 15 of possible African-American nominees for the U.S. Senate.

News: An item about a U.S. House race in South Carolina in the Washington briefs in some editions June 21 misstated the current job of candidate Paul Thurmond. He is a member of the Charleston County Council.

Sports: A cover story June 21 about player attitudes on umpiring and instant replay mischaracterized Frank Robinson's new position with Major League Baseball. He has been named senior vice president of major league operations on a permanent basis.

Money: In a story June 16 on traveling with the iPad, graphic designer Joe Zeff was misidentified.

Opinion: The photo of an abused dog accompanying a June 14 Forum piece was taken by People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals. Santana, the dog pictured, was found by PETA's field staff in North Carolina in 2006. He had suffered untreated life-threatening injuries from dog fighting and had to be euthanized.

News: A 1a graphic on oil spills June 8 misstated how often spills occurred since the 1970s. The number of spills shown was the average per year during each decade.

News: A Texas item in the June 7 Across the USA named an incorrect city. Camp Lucy, a venue for weddings and other events, opened in Dripping Springs.

News: A graphic June 1 about tensions on the Korean Peninsula should have clarified that the number of helicopters listed in the region refer only to attack helicopters.

Money: The Your Money column June 1 should have made clear that credit card issuers are required by law to disclose information about annual fees, balance transfer fees and other fees in credit card solicitations.

Sports: A caption with a photo June 1, keying to a story about the significance the World Cup in South Africa holds for some African-American members of the U.S. team, misidentified the player shown.He is Maurice Edu.

News: A 1A story June 2 on Christians in Iraq misstated the era in which Assyrian Christianity began. It was in the 1st century A.D.

Money: A photo caption on 2B June 1 should have identified the Briggs & Riley Travelware bag as an Exchange 26 Duffle, which is too large to carry on an aircraft.

Life: Credit lines for photos from Michigan's Boyne Highland Resort and the Inn at Bay Harbor in the May 28 Life on Vacation feature were inadvertently reversed.

News: A story May 28 should have made clear that the Coalition for the Homeless supports a New York City plan that would require people with jobs who live in homeless shelters to save some of their pay.

Money: A May 26 story about heavy-duty pickups should have said the 2011 GMC Sierra HD diesel engine will offer 397 horsepower, 7 hp more than Ford's rival Super Duty, and 765 lb.-ft. of torque, 30 more than the Ford.

News: A story May 27 should have made clear that the Coalition for the Homeless supports a New York City plan that would require people with jobs who live in homeless shelters to save some of their pay.

Life: Samantha Bee's new book is called I Know I Am, But What Are You? The title was incorrect in a story May 27.

Money: A May 26 story on NBA licensed merchandise should have said that retail sales of all licensed merchandise based on sports properties fell 17% last year, according to The Licensing Letter.

News: In a story about the end of the TV show Law & Order in some editions May 25, the wrong name was given for the Manhattan district attorney who was the basis of the character played by Steven Hill. It was Robert Morgenthau. In the same story in some editions, the wrong year was given for a photo of actors Anthony Anderson and Jeremy Sisto. It was taken during filming of an episode in 2009.

News: In some editions May 21, a story about the November election misstated the year the Civil Rights Act of 1964 was enacted.

News: A story May 19 about seafood safety in the Gulf of Mexico should have specified that fishing has been banned in only some offshoreareas from Louisiana to the Florida Panhandle.

Life: A story May 21 about fall TV incorrectly stated CW's Gossip Girl's status for fall. It is not moving to a new night.

Sports: A news brief May 20 about reports that TNT analyst Doug Collins was negotiating with the Philadelphia 76ers about their head coaching position misidentified that team as one of the three he had previously coached. The teams were the Chicago Bulls, Detroit Pistons and Washington Wizards.

News: A map of the United States accompanying a May 21 cover story on summer travel plans misidentified the location of Kansas City. It is on Missouri's western border.

Money: A May 18 story on government gas mileage ratings for the 2011 Ford Fiesta should have said the manual transmission model has been certified at 28 miles per gallon in city driving, 37 on the highway.

Sports: A story May 19 included an incorrect date for Dick Vitale's cancer gala in Sarasota, Fla. It is May 21.

Sports: A May 18 news brief about the second stage of the Amgen Tour of California referred incorrectly to the team of cyclist Levi Leipheimer. He rides for Team Radio Shack.

Sports: A May 18 story about Colorado Rockies pitcher Ualdo Jimenez misstated his 2010 salary. It is $1.25 million.

Sports: A May 18 story about the Big Ten Conference's annual spring meetings referred incorrectly to the sport that Tubby Smith coaches at Minnesota. It is men's basketball.

Life: In the USA, Jude Morgan's novel about the Bronte sisters is titled Charlotte and Emily. The British title (A Taste of Sorrow) was given in a story May 12.

News: A May 10 story about the anti-tax Tea Party movement's impact on congressional campaigns incorrectly stated the occupations of two Utah Republicans running for the U.S. Senate. Mike Lee is a lawyer and Tim Bridgewater is a businessman.

Sports: The sport coached by Tubby Smith at the University of Minnesota was incorrect in the May 18 issue. He is the basketball coach.

Money: A May 14 cover story listed an incorrect title for Christine Frietchen of Consumer-Search.com, which aggregates consumer reviews. She is editor in chief.

Sports: A May 12 story about Tiger Woods' former swing coaches misidentified the source of comments made by Butch Harmon. The remarks were from an interview with the PGA Tour Network on Sirius XM Radio.

Sports: A May 11 Deals column reversed in some editions the identities of two players involved in an NFL trade. The Dallas Cowboys acquired Alex Barron from the St. Louis Rams for Bobby Carpenter.

News: Income taxes consumed 9.2% of personal income in 2009, the lowest percentage since 1950. A USA TODAY story on May 11 incorrectly stated that the figure also included sales and property taxes. When sales and property taxes are included, taxes consumed 17.7% of income, the lowest percentage since 1942.

News: A story May 12 about the case of pediatrician Earl Bradley, who is accused of raping or abusing at least 103 children, incorrectly attributed the fact that 50 girls were allegedly abused between December 2008 and December 2009. The number came from a review of the indictments. Also, there are two indictments with 529 counts in the case. Those numbers were incorrect May 12.

Sports: A photo caption that accompanied an article about Alaska's King of the Hill snowboarding contest in the April 9 edition misidentified the snowboarder. He was Kenny Jones.

Money: A cover story May 11 about small business software misstated the subscription rate for Google Apps Premier Edition. The correct price is $50 a year per employee.

Sports: A caption with a photo May 7 from the October UFC bout between Mauricio "Shogun" Rua and Lyoto "The Dragon" Machida reversed the identities of the two fighters. Rua was shown on the right, landing a blow to Machida's head

News: In some May 10 editions, a story about President Obama's nomination of Solicitor General Elena Kagan to the Supreme Court misstated the period since a justice without prior judicial experience has served. Kagan would be the first appointee in nearly 40 years who was not previously a judge.

Sports: A package of photos May 5 related to the alleged beating death of a University of Virginia women's lacrosse player included an incorrect image of the building that housed her off-campus apartment.

News: A story May 5 about a U.S. military base in Okinawa misstated the number of U.S. servicemembers who died on the island in World War II. About 12,000 servicemembers died.

News: Illegal immigration declined from 2007 to 2008, according to the Department of Homeland Security. The headline on a chart accompanying a story May 4 on Arizona's new immigration law misstated the trend.

Sports: A Snapshot May 4 about states that have hosted the most Golden Gloves championship tournaments omitted the time frame for the rankings. The figures referred to events since 1964.

News: A story in April 30 editions about a new campaign finance bill should have said limits on what individuals can contribute to national political parties would remain intact. But the proposal would ease restrictions on when political parties can spend their money to aid candidates.

Sports: A story May 3 rounding up weekend NHRA racing results misidentified the winner in the Pro Stock Motorcycle category. Michael Phillips won that event.

Money: An item in a May 3 edition on the PBS Frontline program titled College Inc. should have said that revenue for the University of Phoenix neared $4 billion last year.
